mid-15c., of opinions, faith, from Late Latin orthodoxus, from Greek orthodoxos "having the right opinion," from orthos "right, true, straight" (see ortho-) + doxa "opinion, praise," from dokein "to seem," from PIE root *dek- "to take, accept" (see decent).

meanings.definitionCn: 劫掠， 掠夺， 破坏

morphology.mnemonic: 向下/移除/取消 + pred + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 15c., from Middle French déprédation, from Late Latin depraedationem (nominative depraedatio) "a plundering," from past participle stem of Latin depraedari "to pillage," from de- "thoroughly" (see de-) + praedari "to plunder," literally "to make prey of," from praeda "prey" (see prey (n.))..
late 15c., embushe, from the English verb or from Middle French embusche, from Old French embuscher (see ambush (v.)).

morphology.mnemonic: beloved person, admired woman + …的场所/与…有关

etymology.story: late 14c., juelrye "precious ornaments, jewel work," from Old French juelerye, from jouel (see jewel).

meanings.definitionCn: 获得； 通用， 流行

etymology.story: early 15c., from Middle French obtenir "acquire, obtain" (14c.), from Latin obtinere "hold, hold fast, take hold of, get possession of, acquire," from ob "to" (though perhaps intensive in this case; see ob-) + tenere "to hold" (see tenet).
1590s, after French homme d'état; see state (n.1) + man (n.).
1550s, from Middle French quadruple (13c.), from Latin quadruplus "fourfold," from quadri- "four" (see quadru-) + -plus "-fold" (see -plus)..
"an expert," especially "one who is skilled in the secrets of anything," 1660s, from Latin adeptus (see adept (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 好奇心， 求知欲； 罕见而有趣之物， 珍品

etymology.story: late 14c., "careful attention to detail," also "desire to know or learn" (originally usually in a bad sense), from Old French curiosete "curiosity, avidity, choosiness" (Modern French curiosité), from Latin curiositatem (nominative curiositas) "desire of knowledge, inquisitiveness," from curiosus (see curious).
"agile, light-footed," c.

meanings.definitionCn: 可爱的； 友好的， 和蔼的， 亲切的

etymology.story: mid-14c., from Old French amiable, from Late Latin amicabilis "friendly," from amicus "friend," related to amare "to love" (see Amy).

meanings.definitionCn: 牺牲， 献身； 献祭， 祭品
特定的；独特的；详细的；挑剔的

morphology.mnemonic: 平等 + 与…有关的/像…的

meanings.definitionCn: 军团， 兵团； 特种部队； 一群人， 一组人

etymology.story: late 13c., cors "body," from Old French cors "body, person, corpse, life" (9c.), from Latin corpus "body" (see corporeal).

meanings.definitionCn: 正规的， 正式的； 形式的

etymology.story: late 14c., "pertaining to form or arrangement;" also, in philosophy and theology, "pertaining to the form or essence of a thing," from Old French formal, formel "formal, constituent" (13c.) and directly from Latin formalis, from forma "a form, figure, shape" (see form (n.)).
Old English renboga; see rain (n.) + bow (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 导师， 讲师； 教练， 指导员

etymology.story: mid-15c., from Old French instructeur (14c.) and directly from Medieval Latin instructor "teacher" (in classical Latin, "preparer"), agent noun from instruere "arrange; inform, teach" (see instruct)..

mid-14c., "warmth or glow of feeling," from Old French fervor "heat; enthusiasm, ardor, passion" (12c., Modern French ferveur), from Latin fervor "a boiling, violent heat; passion, ardor, fury," from fervere "to boil; be hot" (see brew (v.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 富足的， 充足的， 丰富的； 宽敞的

etymology.story: mid-15c., from Middle French ample, from Latin amplus "large, spacious," related to ampla "handle, grip.".

meanings.definitionCn: 内聚力； 凝聚力； 团结， 结合

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + _here + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1670s, from French cohésion, from Latin cohaesionem (nominative cohaesio) "a sticking together," noun of action from past participle stem of cohaerere "to stick together" (see cohere)..

meanings.definitionCn: 招待， 款待； 使快乐， 娱乐

morphology.mnemonic: 之间/相互 + 持有/保持

etymology.story: late 15c., "to keep up, maintain, to keep (someone) in a certain frame of mind," from Middle French entretenir, from Old French entretenir "hold together, stick together, support" (12c.), from entre- "among" (from Latin inter; see inter-) + tenir "to hold" (from Latin tenere; see tenet).

meanings.definitionCn: 缓解， 减轻； 使轻松， 使宽慰； 调剂； 接替， 换班

etymology.story: late 14c., "alleviate (pain, etc.), mitigate; afford comfort; allow respite; diminish the pressure of," also "give alms to, provide for;" also figuratively, "take heart, cheer up;" from Old French relever "to raise, relieve" (11c.) and directly from Latin relevare "to raise, alleviate, lift up, free from a burden," from re-, intensive prefix (see re-), + levare "to lift up, lighten," from levis "not heavy" (see lever).

early 15c., from Old French condiment (13c.), from Latin condimentum "spice, seasoning, sauce," from condire "to preserve, pickle, season," variant of condere "to put away, store," from com- "together" (see com-) + -dere comb.

meanings.definitionCn: 确定， 确信； 必然性

etymology.story: early 15c., from Middle French certitude "certainty" (16c.), from Late Latin certitudinem (nominative certitudo) "that which is certain," from Latin certus "sure, certain" (see certain)..
late 14c., from Old French exceder (14c.) "exceed, surpass, go too far," from Latin excedere "depart, go beyond, be in excess, surpass," from ex- "out" (see ex-) + cedere "go, yield" (see cede).

meanings.definitionCn: 欺骗性的， 导致误解的

etymology.story: 1610s, from French deceptif (late 14c.), from Medieval Latin deceptivus, from decept-, past participle stem of Latin decipere (see deceive).
1580s; see mathematic + -ics.

etymology.story: "one that reacts," 1835, agent noun in Latin form from react.
also cook-out, 1930, American English, from cook (v.) + out (adv.)..

meanings.definitionCn: 标本， 样本； 范例； 抽样

etymology.story: 1610s, "pattern, model," from Latin specimen "indication, mark, example, sign, evidence; that by which a thing is known, means of knowing," from specere "to look at" (see scope (n.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: 外来的， 来自异国的； 奇异的

etymology.story: 1590s, "belonging to another country," from Middle French exotique (16c.) and directly from Latin exoticus, from Greek exotikos "foreign," literally "from the outside," from exo "outside" (see exo-).

meanings.definitionCn: 缓解， 减轻， 轻松； 援救， 救济； 宽慰； 浮雕
联合抵制， 拒绝购买

etymology.story: late 14c., "act of bringing into existence," from Old French introduccion (14c.) and directly from Latin introductionem (nominative introductio) "a leading in," noun of action from past participle stem of introducere "to lead in, bring in; introduce; found, establish; bring forward (as an assertion)," from intro- "inward, to the inside" (see intro-) + ducere "to lead" (see duke (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 订购， 订阅； 申请， 预订； 捐助， 赞助

etymology.story: early 15c., "to sign at the bottom of a document," from Latin subscribere "write, write underneath, sign one's name; register," also figuratively "assent, agree to, approve," from sub "underneath" (see sub-) + scribere "write" (see script (n.)).

1846, "medical treatment of disease," from Modern Latin therapia, from Greek therapeia "curing, healing, service done to the sick; a waiting on, service," from therapeuein "to cure, treat medically," literally "attend, do service, take care of" (see therapeutic)..
1889, from German Chromosom, coined 1888 by German anatomist Wilhelm von Waldeyer-Hartz (1836-1921), from Latinized form of Greek khroma "color" (see chroma) + -some (3)).

etymology.story: 1630s, "action of bringing to a center," noun of action from verb concentrate (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 速度， 速率； 迅速

etymology.story: early 15c., from Latin velocitatem (nominative velocitas) "swiftness, speed," from velox (genitive velocis) "swift, speedy, rapid, quick," of uncertain origin, perhaps related to vehere "carry" (see vehicle), or from the same root as vigil..

morphology.mnemonic: one of the same kind + 身份/状态/技能

etymology.story: 1530s, from Middle French collègue (16c.), from Latin collega "partner in office," from com- "with" (see com-) + leg-, stem of legare "to choose" (see legate).
1630s, from un- (1) "not" + past participle of adorn (v.)..

meanings.definitionCn: 供认， 招供， 坦白； 供词； 承认

etymology.story: late 14c., "action of confessing," originally in religion, from Old French confession (10c.), from Latin confessionem (nominative confessio) "confession, acknowledgement," noun of action from past participle stem of confiteri (see confess).

mid-14c., "noon," from Old French meridien "of the noon time, midday; the Meridian; southerner" (12c.), and directly from Latin meridianus "of midday, of noon, southerly, to the south," from meridies "noon, south," from meridie "at noon," altered by dissimilation from pre-Latin *medi die, locative of medius "mid-" (see medial (adj.)) + dies "day" (see diurnal).

meanings.definitionCn: 干涉， 干扰， 妨碍

morphology.mnemonic: 之间/相互 + one who goes with another.

etymology.story: formerly also enterfere, mid-15c., "to strike against," from Middle French enterferir "exchange blows, strike each other," from entre- "between" (see entre-) + ferir "to strike," from Latin ferire "to knock, strike," related to Latin forare "to bore, pierce" (see bore (v.), and compare punch (v.), which has both the senses "to hit" and "to make a hole in").

morphology.mnemonic: 统治 + 结果/过程/状态

etymology.story: 1640s, "private rooms for the use of one person within a house," from French appartement (16c.), from Italian appartimento, literally "a separated place," from appartere "to separate," from a "to" (see ad-) + parte "side, place," from Latin partem (see part (n.)).

Old English alecgan "to put down, remit, give up," a Germanic compound (cognates: Gothic uslagjan, Old High German irleccan, German erlegen), from a- "down, aside" + lecgan "to lay" (see lay (v.)).

early 15c., in Scottish, from Middle French domaine "domain, estate," from Old French demaine "lord's estate," from Latin dominium "property, dominion," from dominus "lord, master, owner," from domus "house" (see domestic).

etymology.story: "powerful, having influence," 1650s, from Medieval Latin influentialis, from influentia (see influence (n.)).

late 14c., "quality of being pleasant or agreeable," from Old French amenite, from Latin amoenitatem (nominative amoenitas) "delightfulness, pleasantness," from amoenus "pleasant," perhaps related to amare "to love" (see Amy)..

Old English becweðan "to say, speak to, exhort, blame," also "leave by will;" from be- + cweðan "to say," from Proto-Germanic *kwethan, from PIE *gwet- "to say, speak."

Original sense of "say, utter" died out 13c., leaving legal sense of "transfer by will." Closely related to bequest.

meanings.definitionCn: 显然的， 明显的； 表面上的， 貌似的

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French aparant "evident, obvious, visible," from Latin apparentem (nominative apparens) "visible, manifest," present participle of apparere (see appear).

etymology.story: mid-14c., "book of church services," from Anglo-French jurnal, from Old French jornel, "a day; time; a day's travel or work" (12c., Modern French journal), properly "that which takes place daily," noun use of adjective meaning "daily, of the day," from Late Latin diurnalis "daily" (see diurnal).

early 14c., "to cover (a street) with stones or other material," from Old French paver "to pave" (12c.), perhaps a back-formation from Old French pavement or else from Vulgar Latin *pavare, from Latin pavire "to beat, ram, tread down," from PIE *pau- "to cut, strike, stamp" (source also of Latin putare "to prune;" Greek paiein "to strike;" Lithuanian piauju "to cut," piuklas "saw").
early 15c., from Old French distinctif and directly from Medieval Latin distinctivus, from Latin distinct-, past participle of distinguere (see distinguish).
1713, "in a state of primordial chaos," irregularly formed in English from chaos + -ic, probably on model of eros/erotic, demos/demotic, hypnos/hypnotic, etc.

meanings.definitionCn: 吹嘘， 自夸， 自吹自擂

etymology.story: late 14c., "pomp; arrogance, pride;" see brag (v.); the exact relationship of the forms is uncertain.
"vexed, peeved, offended," late 13c., past participle adjective from annoy (v.)..
1738, from a Bantu language of Angola (compare Tshiluba  kivili-chimpenze "ape").

meanings.definitionCn: 习得， 获得； 收购， 购置

etymology.story: late 14c., "act of obtaining," from Old French acquisicion (13c.) or directly from Latin acquisitionem (nominative acquisitio), noun of action from past participle stem of acquirere "get in addition, accumulate," from ad- "extra" (see ad-) + quaerere "to seek to obtain" (see query (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 严格的； 刚硬的， 不易弯曲的； 死板的， 刻板的； 僵硬的

etymology.story: early 15c., from Latin rigidus "hard, stiff, rough, severe," from rigere "be stiff," from PIE *reig- "stretch (tight), bind tightly, make fast" (source also of Old Irish riag "torture," Middle High German ric "band, string").

meanings.definitionCn: 最后期限， 截止时间

etymology.story: "time limit," 1920, American English newspaper jargon, from dead (adj.) + line (n.).
mid-15c., from so (adv.) + past participle of call (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 矿， 矿井； 地雷， 水雷

etymology.story: Old English min "mine, my," (pronoun and adjective), from Proto-Germanic *minaz (source also of Old Frisian, Old Saxon Old High German min, Middle Dutch, Dutch mijn, German mein, Old Norse minn, Gothic meins "my, mine"), from the base of me.

meanings.definitionCn: 到来， 来临； 出现

etymology.story: "important arrival," 1742, an extended sense of  Advent "season before Christmas" (Old English), from Latin adventus "a coming, approach, arrival," in Church Latin "the coming of the Savior," from past participle stem of advenire "arrive, come to," from ad- "to" (see ad-) + venire "to come" (see venue).
1827, "pertaining to or suggestive of the Middle Ages," coined in English from Latin medium "the middle" (see medium (n.)) + aevum "age" (see eon)..

meanings.definitionCn: 全体职工；行政人员；棍棒
为…配备人员； 任职于
包含； 牵涉， 牵连； 使参加

etymology.story: late 14c., "envelop, surround; make cloudy or obscure," from Old French involver and directly from Latin involvere "envelop, surround, overwhelm," literally "roll into," from in- "in" (see in- (2)) + volvere "to roll" (see volvox).
1640s, "tangled," from past participle adjective from complicate.

morphology.mnemonic: greatest premise, greatest among propositions + ium

etymology.story: common European bird, Old English finc "finch," from Proto-Germanic *finkiz "finch" (source also of Middle Low German and Middle Dutch vinke, Dutch vink, Old High German finco, German Fink), perhaps imitative of the bird's note (compare Breton pint "chaffinch," Russian penka "wren")..

meanings.definitionCn: 教学大纲， 课程提纲

etymology.story: 1650s, "table of contents of a series of lectures, etc.," from Late Latin syllabus "list," ultimately a misreading of Greek sittybos "parchment label, table of contents," of unknown origin.

mid-15c., from Latin fragrantem (nominative fragrans) "sweet-smelling," present participle of fragrare "smell strongly, emit (a sweet) odor," from Proto-Italic *fragro-, from PIE root *bhrag- "to smell" (source also of Old Irish broimm "break wind," Middle High German bræhen "to smell," Middle Dutch bracke, Old High German braccho "hound, setter;" see brach).

morphology.mnemonic: 忍受/容忍 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: 1784, "free from bigotry or severity in judging others," from French tolérant (16c.), and directly from Latin tolerantem (nominative tolerans), present participle of tolerare "to bear, endure, tolerate" (see toleration).

1570s, "form of speech of a region or group," from Middle French dialecte, from Latin dialectus "local language, way of speaking, conversation," from Greek dialektos "talk, conversation, speech;" also "the language of a country, dialect," from dialegesthai "converse with each other," from dia- "across, between" (see dia-) + legein "speak" (see lecture (n.))..

late 14c., from Old French rigor "strength, hardness" (13c., Modern French rigueur), from Latin rigorem (nominative rigor) "numbness, stiffness, hardness, firmness; roughness, rudeness," from rigere "be stiff" (see rigid)..

meanings.definitionCn: 模糊的；暗淡的；朦胧的；不乐观的

etymology.story: early 15c., from Latin impellere "to push, strike against; set in motion, drive forward, urge on," from assimilated form of in- "into, in, on, upon" (see in- (2)) + pellere "to push, drive" (see pulse (n.1)).

morphology.mnemonic: that which is taken out, + 与…有关的/像…的 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: 1580s, "fit to be an example," from Middle French exemplaire, from Late Latin exemplaris "that serves as an example, pattern, or motto," from exemplum "example, pattern, model" (see example).

meanings.definitionCn: 学院； 全体教职员工； 能力

meanings.definitionCn: 坚定不移的， 坚决的； 固执的

etymology.story: mid-14c., from Old French adamant and directly from Latin adamantem (nominative adamas) "adamant, hardest iron, steel," also figuratively, of character, from Greek adamas (genitive adamantos) "unbreakable, inflexible" metaphoric of anything unalterable, also the name of a hypothetical hardest material, perhaps literally "invincible," from a- "not" + daman "to conquer, to tame" (see tame (adj.)), or else a word of foreign origin altered to conform to Greek.

meanings.definitionCn: 水力的， 液压的； 液压驱动的； 与水力系统有关的

late 14c., from lime (n.1) + stone (n.)..

1520s, from live (adj.) + stock (n.2)..
"rock that falls to earth, after streaking across the sky as a meteor," 1818, from meteor + -ite..
late 13c., from archaic past participle of Old English meltian, a class III strong verb (see melt (v.))..

late 14c., a re-Latinizing of earlier ampayre, apeyre "make worse, cause to deteriorate" (c.

late 15c., from Old French sumptueux or directly from Latin sumptuosus "costly, very expensive; lavish, wasteful," from sumptus, past participle of sumere "to borrow, buy, spend, eat, drink, consume, employ, take, take up," contraction of *sub-emere, from sub- "under" (see sub-) + emere "to take, buy" (see exempt (adj.)).

late 14c., "capable of being dissolved," from Old French soluble "expungable, eradicable" (13c.), from Late Latin solubilis "that may be loosened or dissolved," from stem of Latin solvere "loosen, dissolve" (see solve).
"changed," early 15c., past participle adjective from vary (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 引起兴趣的， 吸引人的

etymology.story: 1680s, "plotting, scheming," present-participle adjective from intrigue (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 用带捆扎， 束牢； 用绷带包扎

morphology.mnemonic: different in character or quality. + 使…化/造成

etymology.story: late 15c., from Old French diversifier (13c.) "to make diverse," from Medieval Latin diversificare, from Latin diversus (see diverse).
1878, shortening of graphic formula (see graphic).

late 14c., from Old French imbiber, embiber "to soak into," and directly from Latin imbibere "absorb, drink in, inhale," from assimilated form of in- "into, in, on, upon" (see in- (2)) + bibere "to drink," related to potare "to drink," from PIE *po(i)- "to drink" (see potion).

meanings.definitionCn: 不可渗透的， 不透水的

etymology.story: 1690s, from French imperméable or directly from Late Latin impermeabilis, from assimilated form of in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + permeabilis (see permeable).

morphology.mnemonic: 在…之前/预先 + caut + 状态/行为/过程 + …的场所/与…有关

etymology.story: 1720, from precaution + -ary..

1836, from French prétentieux (17c.), from prétention "pretension," from Medieval Latin pretentionem (nominative pretentio) "pretension," noun of action from past participle stem of Latin praetendere (see pretend (v.))..

Middle English hondlen, handlen, "touch with the hands, hold in the hands, fondle, pet," also "to deal with, treat, manhandle," from Old English handlian "to touch or move with the hands," also "deal with, discuss;" formed from hand (n.), perhaps with a frequentative suffix, as fondle from fond.

meanings.definitionCn: 有溶解力的； 有偿付能力的

morphology.mnemonic: 太阳/独自 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: "substance able to dissolve other substances," 1670s, from Latin solventem (see solvent (n.))..
"nude figure in visual art," 1708, from French nud, obsolete variant of nu "naked, nude, bare," from Latin nudus (see nude (adj.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 分散注意力， 使分心

etymology.story: mid-14c., "to draw asunder or apart, to turn aside" (literal and figurative), from Latin distractus, past participle of distrahere "draw in different directions," from dis- "away" (see dis-) + trahere "to draw" (see tract (n.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: 上腭； 味觉， 品尝力

etymology.story: late 14c., "roof of the mouth," from Old French palat and directly from Latin palatum "roof of the mouth," perhaps of Etruscan origin [Klein].

1743, borrowed (during an outbreak of the disease in Europe), from Italian influenza "influenza, epidemic," originally "visitation, influence (of the stars)," from Medieval Latin influentia in the astrological sense (see influence).

meanings.definitionCn: 贷款；出借，借出；暂借的东西

etymology.story: mid-13c., from Old Norse lan, related to lja "to lend," from Proto-Germanic *laikhwniz (source also of Old Frisian len "thing lent," Middle Dutch lene, Dutch leen "loan, fief," Old High German lehan, German Lehn "fief, feudal tenure"), originally "to let have, to leave (to someone)," from PIE *leikw- "to leave" (see relinquish).

meanings.definitionCn: 模拟的， 虚拟的； 实际上的， 事实上的

etymology.story: late 14c., "influencing by physical virtues or capabilities, effective with respect to inherent natural qualities," from Medieval Latin virtualis, from Latin virtus "excellence, potency, efficacy," literally "manliness, manhood" (see virtue).

meanings.definitionCn: 挤压；挤入；挤过，塞入；压榨，榨取；
无止境的， 无穷尽的

etymology.story: Old English endeleas "boundless, eternal;" see end (n.) + -less.
"hit repeatedly," from Middle English pounen, from Old English punian "crush, pulverize, beat, bruise," from West Germanic *puno- (source also of Low German pun, Dutch puin "fragments").

morphology.mnemonic: to bury + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: mid-15c., "action of planting," from Middle French plantation, from Latin plantationem (nominative plantatio) "a planting," noun of action from past participle stem of plantare "to plant" (see plant).

meanings.definitionCn: 忽视； 不顾， 不理

etymology.story: 1610s, "not to know, to be ignorant of," from French ignorer "be unaware of" (14c.), or directly from Latin ignorare "not to know, be unacquainted; take no notice of, disregard" (see ignorant).

root.meaning: range of something, place or scene of activity

morphology.mnemonic: hemi + range of something, place or scene of activity

etymology.story: late 14c., hemysperie, in reference to the celestial sphere, from Late Latin hemisphaerium, from Greek hemisphairion, from hemi- "half" (see hemi-) + sphaira "sphere" (see sphere).

meanings.definitionCn: 延长， 延续， 持续； 延长物， 扩建物； 延续部分

morphology.mnemonic: to hang together + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French continuation (13c.), or directly from Latin continuationem (nominative continuatio), noun of action from continuat-, past participle stem of continuare (see continue)..

meanings.definitionCn: 鼓掌， 喝彩； 称赞

morphology.mnemonic: moon月亮 + 与…有关的/像…的

etymology.story: "crescent-shaped," early 15c.; "pertaining to the moon," 1620s, from Old French lunaire (15c.), from Latin lunaris "of the moon," from luna "moon" (with capital L- "moon goddess"); see Luna..

meanings.definitionCn: 诗歌； 诗集； 诗意

etymology.story: late 14c., "poetry; a poem; ancient literature; poetical works, fables, or tales," from Old French poetrie (13c.), and perhaps directly from Medieval Latin poetria (c.650), from Latin poeta (see poet).

late 14c., from Old French arrogant (14c.), from Latin arrogantem (nominative arrogans) "assuming, overbearing, insolent," present participle of arrogare "to claim for oneself, assume" (see arrogance).

meanings.definitionCn: 转播； 传达， 转述
接替人员， 轮换者； 接力赛； 中继设备
允许， 同意； 许可证， 书面许可

etymology.story: early 15c., from Latin permissionem (nominative permissio), noun of action from past participle stem of permittere (see permit (v.))..
1650s, from Latin permeatus, past participle of permeare "to pass through" (see permeable).

meanings.definitionCn: 一夫一妻制的； 单配的

morphology.mnemonic: 单一 + 婚姻 + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: "having but one wife or husband at a time," 1770, from Medieval Latin monogamus, from Greek monogamos "marrying only once" (see monogamy)..

meanings.definitionCn: 时刻表， 日程表； 清单， 明细表
逮捕； 领会， 理解

etymology.story: mid-14c., "to grasp in the senses or mind," from Old French aprendre (12c.) "teach; learn; take, grasp; acquire," or directly from Latin apprehendere "to take hold of, grasp," from ad- "to" + prehendere "to seize" (see prehensile).

meanings.definitionCn: 令人震惊的； 严重损害身体的； 极其有害的

etymology.story: 1530s, "hard to accomplish, difficult to do," from Latin arduus "high, steep," also figuratively, "difficult," from PIE root *eredh- "to grow, high" (see ortho-).

meanings.definitionCn: 解释， 说明； 理解； 翻译

etymology.story: late 14c., "expound the meaning of, render clear or explicit," from Old French interpreter "explain; translate" (13c.) and directly from Latin interpretari "explain, expound, understand," from interpres "agent, translator," from inter- "between" (see inter-) + second element of uncertain origin, perhaps related to Sanskrit prath- "to spread abroad," PIE *per- (5) "to traffic in, sell" (see pornography).

meanings.definitionCn: 派别， 派系， 小集团； 派系斗争

morphology.mnemonic: 事实 + 状态/行为/过程

meanings.definitionCn: 海滩， 湖滩， 河滩

etymology.story: 1580s, "the whole world, cosmos, the totality of existing things," from Old French univers (12c.), from Latin universum "all things, everybody, all people, the whole world," noun use of neuter of adjective universus "all together, all in one, whole, entire, relating to all," literally "turned into one," from unus "one" (see one) + versus, past participle of vertere "to turn" (see versus)..

meanings.definitionCn: 给予，授予；允许，同意；承认
拨款； 津贴； 授予物

etymology.story: in early use also graunt, early 13c., "to allow, permit (something); consent to (a prayer, request, etc.)," from Old French graanter, variant of creanter "assure, promise, guarantee, swear; confirm, authorize, approve (of)," from Latin credentem (nominative credens), present participle of credere "to believe, to trust" (see credo).
1620s, from French recouper "to cut back" (12c.), from Old French re- "back" (see re-) + couper "to cut," from coup "a blow" (see coup).
1897, coined from Latin aqui-, comb.

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + 悬挂/称量/支付

etymology.story: late 14c., "to belong to as a possession or right," from Old French apendre (13c.) belong, be dependent (on); attach (oneself) to; hang, hang up," and directly from Latin appendere "to cause to hang (from something), weigh," from ad- "to" (see ad-) + pendere "hang" (see pendant).
also news-letter , 1670s, from news (n.) + letter (n.).
1927, from co- + pilot (n.)..

1620s, "intensely cold," from Latin frigidus "cold, chill, cool," figuratively "indifferent," also "flat, dull, trivial," from stem of frigere "be cold;" related to noun frigus "cold, coldness, frost," from Proto-Italic *srigos-, from PIE root *srig- "cold" (source also of Greek rhigos "cold, frost").

meanings.definitionCn: 遗物， 遗迹， 遗风

etymology.story: early 13c., "body part or other object from a holy person," from Old French relique (11c., plural reliques), from Late Latin reliquiæ (plural) "remains of a martyr," in classical Latin "remains, remnants," noun use of fem.

meanings.definitionCn: 把…打折扣； 不全信， 漠视， 低估

etymology.story: 1620s, "reckon as an abatement or deduction," from Old French desconter (13c., Modern French décompter), from Medieval Latin discomputare, from dis- (see dis-) + computare "to count" (see count (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 改变； 转变， 转化
超凡魅力； 感召力； 号召力

etymology.story: "gift of leadership, power of authority," c.

meanings.definitionCn: 除， 除法； 分开， 分隔； 分配； 部分； 分歧， 分裂； 部门

morphology.mnemonic: watershed, separation between river valleys + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French division, from Latin divisionem (nominative divisio), from divid-, stem of dividere (see divide).

meanings.definitionCn: 天平， 秤； 平衡； 差额； 余款
偷偷接近； 跟踪； 趾高气扬地走

etymology.story: "stem of a plant," early 14c., probably a diminutive (with -k suffix) of stale "one of the uprights of a ladder, handle, stalk," from Old English stalu "wooden part" (of a tool or instrument), from Proto-Germanic *stalla- (source also of Old English steala "stalk, support," steall "place"), from PIE *stol-no-, suffixed form of *stol-, variant of root *stel- "to put, stand" (see stall (n.1)).

1570s, from Latinized form of Greek pankreas "sweetbread (pancreas as food), pancreas," literally "entirely flesh," from pan- "all" (see pan-) + kreas "flesh" (see raw), probably on notion of homogeneous substance of the organ..

meanings.definitionCn: 供应者， 供应商， 供货方
家长的， 族长的； 德高望重的
音高，音调；倾斜度；沥青，柏油；

etymology.story: mid-14c., "action, deeds," from Anglo-French fet, from Old French fait "action, deed, achievement" (12c.), from Latin factum "thing done," a noun based on the past participle of facere "make, do" (see factitious, and compare fact).

morphology.mnemonic: 不/进入 + 走/让步 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: mid-15c., from Old French *incessant or directly from Late Latin incessantem (nominative incessans) "unceasing," from Latin in- "not" (see in- (1)) + cessans, present participle of cessare "to cease" (see cease (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 洪流， 急流； 爆发， 迸发
欣赏； 理解， 体谅； 感激； 鉴定， 评价

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + 价格/价值 + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French statue, estatue "(pagan) statue, graven image" (12c.), from Latin statua "image, statue, monumental figure, representation in metal," properly "that which is set up," back-formation from statuere "to cause to stand, set up," from status "a standing, position" (see status).

meanings.definitionCn: 感官，官能；感觉；判断力；意义，意思
酿造；冲泡；酝酿，行将发生
要求， 主张； 自命不凡； 虚饰

etymology.story: mid-15c., "assertion, allegation; objection; intention; signification," from Medieval Latin pretensionem (nominative praetensio), noun of action from past participle stem of Latin praetendere "stretch in front, put forward, allege" (see pretend (v.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 向下/移除/取消 + 弯曲

etymology.story: 1550s, from Latin deflectere "to bend (something) aside or downward," from de- "away" (see de-) + flectere "to bend" (see flexible).

meanings.definitionCn: 有道德的， 品行端正的， 品德高的； 〈贬〉自命清高的， 自以为是的
无效的； 无可靠根据的， 站不住脚的

root.meaning: sufficiently supported by facts or authority, well-grounded

morphology.mnemonic: 不/进入 + sufficiently supported by facts or authority, well-grounded

meanings.definitionCn: 合法的， 法定的； 正当合理的， 合情合理的

etymology.story: "establish the legitimacy of, make lawful," 1590s, from Medieval Latin legitimatus, past participle of legitimare "make lawful" (see legitimate (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 间隔时间； 间距； 幕间休息

etymology.story: early 14c., "time elapsed between two actions or events," from Old French intervalle "interval, interim" (14c.), earlier entreval (13c.) and directly from Late Latin intervallum "a space between, an interval of time, a distance," originally "space between palisades or ramparts" [OED], from inter "between" (see inter-) + vallum "rampart, palisade, wall," which is apparently a collective form of vallus "stake," from PIE *walso- "a post" (see wall (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 出/从 + 强壮/价值 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1831, back-formation from evaluation, or else from French évaluer, back-formation from évaluation.

meanings.definitionCn: 身份， 地位； 情况

etymology.story: 1670s, "height" of a situation or condition, later "legal standing of a person" (1791), from Latin status "condition, position, state, manner, attitude" from past participle stem of stare "to stand," from PIE *ste-tu-, from root *stā- "to stand" (see stet).

meanings.definitionCn: 渗出，慢慢流出；洋溢着，充满

etymology.story: "soft mud," Old English wase "soft mud, mire," from Proto-Germanic *waison (source also of Old Saxon waso "wet ground, mire," Old Norse veisa "pond of stagnant water"), from PIE *wes- (2) "wet." Modern spelling is mid-1500s..

1894, from French sémantique, applied by Michel Bréal (1883) to the psychology of language, from Greek semantikos "significant," from semainein "to show by sign, signify, point out, indicate by a sign," from sema "sign, mark, token; omen, portent; constellation; grave" (Doric sama), from PIE root *dheie- "to see, look" (source also of Sanskrit dhyati "he meditates;" see zen)..

meanings.definitionCn: 心烦意乱的； 不适的
使心烦意乱； 颠覆， 推翻； 搅乱
困扰， 麻烦； 出乎意料的结局； 苦恼
散布， 扩散； 传播

root.meaning: device that breaks an electrical circuit

morphology.mnemonic: 分开/二 + device that breaks an electrical circuit + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., from Latin diffusionem (nominative diffusio) "a pouring forth," noun of action from past participle stem of diffundere "scatter, pour out," from dis- "apart, in every direction" (see dis-) + fundere "pour" (see found (v.2))..

meanings.definitionCn: 曲线； 曲线图； 弯曲
星罗棋布于；打点于；点缀
点， 圆点； 小数点
撇去； 掠过， 擦过； 浏览， 略读
跳；跳过，略过，漏过；不出席

morphology.mnemonic: 不/分离 + semin + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: early 15c., from Old French liquefier "liquefy, dissolve," from Latin liquefacere "make liquid, melt," from liquere "be fluid" (see liquid (adj.)) + facere "to make" (see factitious)..

meanings.definitionCn: 大批离去， 成群外出

etymology.story: late Old English, the second book of the Old Testament, from Latin exodus, from Greek exodos "a military expedition; a solemn procession; departure; death," literally "a going out," from ex- "out" (see ex-) + hodos "way" (see cede).

meanings.definitionCn: 洗衣店， 洗衣房； 洗好的衣服； 待洗的衣服

etymology.story: late 14c., "place for washing;" mid-15c., "act of washing," a contraction (compare launder) of Middle English lavendrie (late 13c.), from Old French lavanderie "wash-house," from Vulgar Latin lavandaria "things to be washed," plural of lavandarium, from lavare "to wash" (see lave).

meanings.definitionCn: 离题， 扯到枝节上； 题外话， 枝节内容

morphology.mnemonic: 分开/二 + 步/走 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., from Latin digressionem (nominative digressio) "a going away, departing," noun of action from past participle stem of digredi "to deviate," from dis- "apart, aside" (see dis-) + gradi "to step, go" (see grade (n.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 勉强做； 不情愿地给
信使； 预示， 预兆
积累， 积聚， 堆积

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + 堆积/积累 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1520s, from Latin accumulatus, past participle of accumulare "to heap up" (see accumulation).

meanings.definitionCn: 联盟， 同盟； 结合， 联合

etymology.story: 1610s, "the growing together of parts," from French coalition (1540s), from Late Latin coalitus "fellowship," originally past participle of Latin coalescere "unite, grow together, become one in growth" (see coalesce).

meanings.definitionCn: 使显得矮小，使相形见绌

etymology.story: mid-13c., "sinfulness," from Old French crimne (12c., Modern French crime), from Latin crimen (genitive criminis) "charge, indictment, accusation; crime, fault, offense," perhaps from cernere "to decide, to sift" (see crisis).

early 14c., leisir, "opportunity to do something," also "free time, time at one's disposal," from Old French leisir, loisir "capacity, ability, freedom (to do something); permission; spare time; free will; idleness, inactivity," noun use of infinitive leisir "be permitted," from Latin licere "to be allowed" (see licence (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 包围， 圈住； 围绕， 环绕

etymology.story: early 15c., "to flood, overflow," from Anglo-French surounder, Middle French soronder "to overflow, abound; surpass, dominate," from Late Latin superundare "overflow," from Latin super "over" (see super-) + undare "to flow in waves," from unda "wave" (see water (n.1); and compare abound).

Old English merran (Anglian), mierran (West Saxon) "to waste, spoil," from Proto-Germanic *marzjan (source also of Old Frisian meria, Old High German marren "to hinder, obstruct," Gothic marzjan "to hinder, offend"), from PIE root *mers- "to trouble, confuse" (source also of Sanskrit mrsyate "forgets, neglects," Lithuanian mirszati "to forget").
Old English foreweard, "the fore or front part" of something, "outpost; scout;" see forward (adv.).

dark brown or black pigment found in animal bodies, 1832, Modern Latin, with chemical suffix -in (2); first element from Greek melas (genitive melanos) "black," from PIE root *mel- (2) "of darkish color" (source also of Sanskrit malinah "dirty, stained, black," Lithuanian melynas "blue," Latin mulleus "reddish").

meanings.definitionCn: 透彻理解， 吸收， 消化； 同化

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + 相似/相同 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: early 15c., from Latin assimilatus "feigned, pretended, fictitious," past participle of assimilare "to make like," from ad- "to" (see ad-) + simulare "make similar," from similis "like, resembling" (see similar).

root.meaning: moral principles of a person or group

morphology.mnemonic: moral principles of a person or group + 与…有关的/形容词

meanings.definitionCn: 狂热的， 热烈的， 充满激情的

etymology.story: "full of zeal" (in the service of a person or cause), 1520s, from Medieval Latin zelosus "full of zeal" (source of Italian zeloso, Spanish celoso), from zelus (see zeal).

meanings.definitionCn: 可比较的， 类似的； 比得上的

etymology.story: early 15c., from Middle French comparable, from Latin comparabilis "capable of comparison," from comparare (see comparison).
"food put on a hook or trap to lure prey," c.
early 14c., from Old French suget, subject (Modern French sujet), from Latin subiectus (see subject (n.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 承认， 认同； 认出， 识别， 辨认； 赏识

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + 知道/认识 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: mid-15c., "knowledge of an event or incident; understanding," from Middle French recognition (15c.) and directly from Latin recognitionem (nominative recognitio) "a reviewing, investigation, examination," noun of action from past participle stem of recognoscere "to acknowledge, know again; examine" (see recognize).

meanings.definitionCn: 无兴趣， 冷漠； 客观， 公正
召集， 召开； 聚集， 集合

etymology.story: early 15c., from Middle French convenir "to suit, agree," from Latin convenire "unite, be suitable, agree, assemble," from com- "together" (see com-) + venire "to come" (see venue).
early 15c., "income from property or possessions," from Middle French revenue, in Old French, "a return," noun use of fem.

meanings.definitionCn: 条， 带状物； 条纹

etymology.story: "long, narrow, flat piece," mid-15c., "narrow piece of cloth," probably related to or from Middle Low German strippe "strap, thong," and from the same source as stripe (n.1).

meanings.definitionCn: 垄断， 专卖； 垄断商品， 专卖商品； 独占， 专利

morphology.mnemonic: 单一 + 多 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: "exclusive control of a commodity or trade," 1530s, from Latin monopolium, from Greek monopolion "right of exclusive sale," from mono- + polein "to sell," from PIE root *pel- (5) "to sell" (source also of Sanskrit panate "barters, purchases," Lithuanian pelnas "gain," Old Church Slavonic splenu, Russian polon "prey, booty," Old Norse falr, Dutch veil, German feil "for sale, venal").

meanings.definitionCn: 商议， 谈判； 流通

morphology.mnemonic: 否定 + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: early 15c., from Old French negociacion "business, trade," and directly from Latin negotiationem (nominative negotiatio) "business, traffic," noun of action from past participle stem of negotiari "carry on business, do business, act as a banker," from negotium "a business, employment, occupation, affair (public or private)," also "difficulty, pains, trouble, labor," literally "lack of leisure," from neg- "not" (see deny) + otium "ease, leisure." The sense expansion from "doing business" to also include "bargaining" about anything took place in Latin..

meanings.definitionCn: 谷物； 颗粒； 纹理； 少量
