etymology.story: early 15c., "to pledge" (something, as security for payment), from Old French engagier "bind (by promise or oath), pledge; pawn" (12c.), from phrase en gage "under pledge," from en "in" (see en- (1)) + gage "pledge," through Frankish from Proto-Germanic *wadiare "pledge" (see wed).

meanings.definitionCn: 精明的， 干练的； 熟练的， 灵巧的

etymology.story: 1650s, "dexterous," originally "rightly," from French adroit, from phrase à droit "according to right," from Old French à "to" (see ad-) + droit "right," from Late Latin directum "right, justice," accusative of Latin directus "straight" (see direct (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 胃口， 食欲； 欲望

etymology.story: 1796, from French frivolité, from Old French frivole "frivolous," from Latin frivolus (see frivolous)..

1810, "art of a general," from French stratégie (18c.) and directly from Greek strategia "office or command of a general," from strategos "general, commander of an army," also the title of various civil officials and magistrates, from stratos "multitude, army, expedition, encamped army," literally "that which is spread out" (see structure (n.)) + agos "leader," from agein "to lead" (see act (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 剩余物， 残余； 其他的人； 差数， 余数

etymology.story: late 14c., from Anglo-French remainder, Old French remaindre, noun use of infinitive, a variant of Old French remanoir (see remain (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 笨拙的； 不得体的， 冒犯人的； 复杂难懂的， 难处理的

etymology.story: 1590s, "acting as if benumbed," alteration of Middle English clumsid "numb with cold" (14c.), past participle of clumsen "to benumb, stiffen or paralyze with cold or fear," from a Scandinavian source akin to Old Norse klumsa "make speechless, palsy; prevent from speaking," intensive of kluma "to make motionless." For insertion of -s-, see flimsy.

meanings.definitionCn: 领土， 版图； 领域， 地盘

etymology.story: late 14c., "land under the jurisdiction of a town, state, etc.," probably from Latin territorium "land around a town, domain, district," from terra "earth, land" (see terrain) + -orium, suffix denoting place (see -ory).

meanings.definitionCn: 传唤， 传讯； 召集， 召开； 鼓起， 振作
摇尾乞怜， 奴颜婢膝

etymology.story: 1590s, Shakespearean back-formation from groveling "on the face, prostrate" (mid-14c.), also used in Middle English as an adjective but probably really an adverb, from gruffe, from Old Norse grufe "prone" + obsolete adverbial suffix -ling (which survives also as the -long in headlong, sidelong).

meanings.definitionCn: 维持， 保持； 维修， 保养； 主张， 坚持； 赡养， 负担

etymology.story: mid-13c., "to practice habitually," from Anglo-French meintenir (Old French maintenir, 12c.) "keep (a wife), sustain; persevere in, practice continually," from Latin manu tenere "hold in the hand," from manu, ablative of manus "hand" (see manual) + tenere "to hold" (see tenet).

meanings.definitionCn: 转移； 改变； 轮班

etymology.story: Old English sciftan, scyftan "arrange, place, order," also "divide, partition; distribute, allot, share," from Proto-Germanic *skiftan (source also of Old Norse skipta "to divide, change, separate," Old Frisian skifta "to decide, determine, test," Dutch schiften "to divide, turn," German schichten "to classify," Schicht "shift").

meanings.definitionCn: 使恢复原状， 修复； 改造， 使恢复正常生活； 恢复…的名誉

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + obtain necessary qualifications

etymology.story: 1570s, "to bring back to a former condition after decay or damage," back-formation from rehabilitation and in part from Medieval Latin rehabilitatus, past participle of rehabilitare.
"quality or state of being an expert," 1868, from French expertise (16c.) "expert appraisal, expert's report," from expert (see expert).

meanings.definitionCn: 特价商品，廉价货；协议；交易

etymology.story: mid-14c., "business transaction or agreement," also "that which is acquired by bargaining," from Old French bargaine, from bargaignier (see bargain (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 选举的； 可选择的； 选修的

etymology.story: early 15c., "voluntary," from Late Latin electivus, from elect-, past participle stem of eligere "to pick out, choose" (see election).
by 1841, from French pivoter and from pivot (n).
1510s, from Middle French dissuader and directly from Latin dissuadere "to advise against, oppose by argument," from dis- "off, against" (see dis-) + suadere "to urge" (see suasion).

meanings.definitionCn: 鬼鬼祟祟的人， 卑鄙者； 运动鞋

etymology.story: 1590s, "one who sneaks," agent noun from sneak (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 确定的， 确凿的， 不容置疑的； 结论性的

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 关闭 + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: 1610s, "occurring at the end," from French conclusif, from Late Latin conclusivus, from conclus-, past participle stem of concludere (see conclude).
1550s, from en- (1) "make, put in" + compass (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 食物； 生计； 维持

morphology.mnemonic: 统治 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: 1911, from German Allergie, coined 1906 by  Austrian pediatrician Clemens E.

meanings.definitionCn: 客观的， 无私的， 公正的； 无兴趣的， 不关心的

etymology.story: 1610s, "unconcerned," the sense we now would ascribe to uninterested, with the sense of "impartial" going to disinteressed (c.
"a wrapper, an enclosing cover," specifically a prepared wrapper for a letter or other paper, 1705, from French enveloppe (13c.), a back-formation from envelopper "to envelop" (see envelop)..
1620s, from Latin depositum, from deponere (see deposit (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 消散， 消失； 挥霍， 浪费

etymology.story: early 15c., from Latin dissipatus, past participle of dissipare "to spread abroad, scatter, disperse; squander, disintegrate," from dis- "apart" (see dis-) + supare "to throw, scatter," from PIE *swep- "to throw, sling, cast" (source also of Lithuanian supu "to swing, rock," Old Church Slavonic supo "to strew").

meanings.definitionCn: 讲述， 描述； 重新清点， 重计

etymology.story: also re-count, "a new count" (especially in an election), 1855, American English, from re- + count (n)..
mid-14c., from Medieval Latin flasco "container, bottle," from Late Latin flasconem (nominative flasco) "bottle," which is of uncertain origin.

morphology.mnemonic: 流动 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: mid-15c., from Old French fluctuacion (12c.) or directly from Latin fluctuationem (nominative fluctuatio) "a wavering, vacillation," noun of action from past participle stem of fluctuare "to undulate, to move in waves," from fluctus "a wave, billow, surge, a flowing," from past participle of fluere "to flow" (see fluent)..

meanings.definitionCn: 易弯曲的， 柔韧的； 易受影响的； 顺从的

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French ploiable "flexible, bendable," from plier "to bend" (see ply (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 打扰； 骚动； 心神不安， 烦恼

etymology.story: late 13c., "mental distress," from Old French destorbance (12c., Old North French distorbance), from destourber, from Latin disturbare (see disturb).

morphology.mnemonic: to share a secret with + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: 1610s, confident, "(male) person trusted with private affairs," from French confident (16c.), from Italian confidente "a trusty friend," literally "confident, trusty," from Latin confidentem (nominative confidens), present participle of confidere "to trust, confide" (see confidence).

morphology.mnemonic: 相似/相同 + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: 1650s, from Medieval Latin simultaneus, perhaps from simultim "at the same time," extended from Latin simul "at the same time" (see similar (adj.)), or from simul with ending abstracted from Late Latin spontaneus, where the -t- is organic.

meanings.definitionCn: 保证， 担保； 保证书
即兴创作； 临时准备

etymology.story: 1808, from Italian improvisare "to sing or speak extempore" (see improvisation), and partly from French improviser.

late 15c., "act of plundering or pillaging," from Latin praedationem (nominative praedatio) "a plundering, act of taking booty," from praedari "to rob, to plunder," from praeda "plunder, booty, prey" (see prey (n.)).

late 14c., "praise, present as worthy," from Medieval Latin recommendare, from Latin re-, here probably an intensive prefix, or else from a sense now obscure (see re-), + commendare "commit to one's care, commend" (see commend).

morphology.mnemonic: 向下/移除/取消 + 移动 + ize

etymology.story: "to send home (troops) as not required for active service," 1876, probably a back-formation from demobilization (q.v.).
Old English leccan "to moisten, water, wet, irrigate" (see leak (v.)).

1530s, "abnormal" (usually in a bad sense), from Latin enormis "out of rule, irregular, shapeless; extraordinary, very large," from assimilated form of ex- "out of" (see ex-) + norma "rule, norm" (see norm), with English -ous substituted for Latin -is.

meanings.definitionCn: 诀窍； 技能， 本领； 习惯， 癖好

etymology.story: mid-14c., "a deception, trick, device," a word of uncertain origin.

meanings.definitionCn: 懒惰的， 懒散的； 不活跃的

etymology.story: 1660s, "causing no pain, painless," from French indolent (16c.) or directly from Late Latin indolentem (see indolence).

late 14c., "either of the two circles in the celestial sphere which describe the northernmost and southernmost points of the ecliptic," from Late Latin tropicus "of or pertaining to the solstice" (as a noun, "one of the tropics"), from Latin tropicus "pertaining to a turn," from Greek tropikos "of or pertaining to a turn or change; of or pertaining to the solstice" (as a noun, "the solstice," short for tropikos kyklos), from trope "a turning" (see trope).

mid-13c., from Anglo-French beverage, Old French bevrage, from Old French boivre "to drink" (Modern French boire; from Latin bibere "to imbibe;" see imbibe) + -age, suffix forming mass or abstract nouns (see -age)..

morphology.mnemonic: 说/讲 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1540s, "a word;" 1580s, "expression of ideas in words," from Late Latin dictionem (nominative dictio) "a saying, expression, word," noun of action from dic-, past participle stem of Latin dicere "speak, tell, say" (source of French dire "to say"), related to dicare "proclaim, dedicate," from PIE root *deik- "to point out" (source also of Sanskrit dic- "point out, show," Greek deiknynai "to show, to prove," Latin digitus "finger," Old High German zeigon, German zeigen "to show," Old English teon "to accuse," tæcan "to teach").

Old English ripe "ready for reaping, fit for eating, mature," from West Germanic *ripijaz (source also of Old Saxon ripi, Middle Dutch ripe, Dutch rijp, Old High German rifi, German reif); related to Old English repan "to reap" (see reap).
"quality ascribed to someone," late 14c., from Latin attributum "anything attributed," noun use of neuter of attributus (see attribute (v.)).
1831, "pertaining to origins," coined by Carlyle as if from Greek genetikos from genesis "origin" (see genesis).
mid-15c., from Old French triumpher (13c.), from Latin triumphare, from triumphus (see triumph (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 唤醒， 唤起； 激起， 引起

etymology.story: 1590s, "awaken" (transitive), from a- (1) "on" + rouse.
Old English nistan "to build nests," from Proto-Germanic *nistijanan, from the source of nest (n.).

1700, thieves' cant, "to act as a lookout, spy on," from Middle English tuten "to peep, peer," probably from a variant of Old English totian "to stick out, peep, peer," from Proto-Germanic *tut- "project" (source also of Dutch tuit "sprout, snout," Middle Dutch tute "nipple, pap," Middle Low German tute "horn, funnel," Old Norse tota "teat, toe of a shoe").

morphology.mnemonic: 出/向外/前任 + propri + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1610s, back-formation from expropriation, or from earlier adjective (mid-15c.), or from Medieval Latin expropriatus, past participle of expropriare "to deprive of one's own."

meanings.definitionCn: 共识， 一致同意； 舆论

etymology.story: 1854 as a term in physiology; 1861 of persons; from Latin consensus "agreement, accord," past participle of consentire (see consent (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 装饰； 对…添枝加叶， 渲染

morphology.mnemonic: 使成为/放入 + 战争 + 有点…/像…

etymology.story: mid-14c., "to render beautiful," from Old French embelliss-, stem of embellir "make beautiful, ornament," from assimilated form of en- (see en- (1)) + bel "beautiful," from Latin bellus "handsome, pretty, fine" (see bene-).
"science of versification," 1760, from Greek he metrike "prosody," plural of metron "meter, a verse; that by which anything is measured; measure, length, size, limit, proportion" (see meter (n.2))..

meanings.definitionCn: 完全的， 绝对的； 地道的； 无疑的， 确凿的； 不受限制的， 不受约束的

etymology.story: late 14c., "unrestricted; complete, perfect;" also "not relative to something else" (mid-15c.), from Middle French absolut (14c., Old French asolu, Modern French absolu), from Latin absolutus, past participle of absolvere "to set free, make separate" (see absolve).

meanings.definitionCn: 收回， 撤销； 缩回

etymology.story: early 15c., "to draw (something) back," from Old French retracter (14c.) and directly from Latin retractus, past participle of retrahere "to draw back" (see retraction).

meanings.definitionCn: 传播流言飞语， 说长道短
闲话，流言飞语；爱说长道短的人

morphology.mnemonic: 技术 + ic + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1620s, in reference to terminology, from technology + -ical.
Old English seolfursmið; see silver (n.) + smith (n.)..

meanings.definitionCn: 剩余物， 残留物； 古代遗物， 遗迹， 遗址； 遗骸

morphology.mnemonic: 谷物，谷粒 + 与…有关的/像…的

etymology.story: 1790, from Late Latin granulum "granule, a little grain," diminutive of Latin granum "grain, seed," from PIE root *gre-no- "grain" (see corn (n.1)) + -ar.

meanings.definitionCn: 纳入， 合并； 包含； 吸收； 注册成立

morphology.mnemonic: 不/进入 + body体（corpor也作corp） + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: late 14c., "to put (something) into the body or substance of (something else), blend; absorb, eat," also "solidify, harden," often in medical writing, from Late Latin incorporatus, past participle of incorporare "unite into one body, embody, include," from Latin in- "into, in, on, upon" (see in- (2)) + verb from corpus (genitive corporis) "body" (see corporeal).
"black lead," 1796, from German Graphit, coined 1789 by German mineralogist Abraham Gottlob Werner (1750-1817) from Greek graphein "write" (see -graphy) + mineral suffix -ite.
early 14c., "to brag, speak arrogantly;" from the same source as boast (n.).
early 15c., "repeat again and again," from Late Latin reiteratus, past participle of reiterare "to repeat," from re- "again" (see re-) + iterare "to repeat," from iterum "again" (see iteration).

meanings.definitionCn: 相对物； 职位相当的人； 副本

etymology.story: mid-15c., originally countre part "duplicate of a legal document," from Middle French contrepartie, from contre "facing, opposite" (see contra-) + partie "copy of a person or thing," originally fem.

meanings.definitionCn: 君主的；至高无上的；独立自主的
君主， 元首， 最高统治者

etymology.story: early 14c., "great, superior, supreme," from Old French soverain "highest, supreme, chief," from Vulgar Latin *superanus "chief, principal" (source also of Spanish soberano, Italian soprano), from Latin super "over" (see super-).

meanings.definitionCn: 葫芦； 葫芦制成的容器
吸引人的， 有魅力的； 引起注意的

etymology.story: late 14c., "absorptive," from Middle French attractif (14c.), from attract-, past participle stem of attrahere (see attract).

meanings.definitionCn: 极好的， 高质量的， 上乘的； 华丽的

etymology.story: 1540s, "noble, magnificent" (of buildings, etc.), from Latin superbus "grand, proud, splendid; haughty, vain, insolent," from super "above, over" (see super-).
1720, "work done by an engineer," from engineer (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 复写； 复制； 使加倍

morphology.mnemonic: du + 折叠/交织 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1620s, "to double," from Latin duplicatus, past participle of duplicare (see duplicate (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 和蔼可亲的， 易于交谈的， 友善的

etymology.story: late 15c., from Old French afable (14c.), from Latin affabilis "approachable, courteous, kind, friendly," literally "who can be (easily) spoken to," from affari "to speak to," from ad- "to" (see ad-) + fari "to speak," from PIE root *bha- (2) "to speak, tell, say" (see fame (n.)).

root.meaning: a fold in a length of cloth

morphology.mnemonic: 向下/移除/取消 + a fold in a length of cloth

etymology.story: late 14c., "detriment, harm;" early 15c.
early 15c., legal, "a charge, a formal accusation," from Old French surmise "accusation," noun use of past participle of surmettre (see surmise (v.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 出/从 + 射线/放射 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: early 15c., "destroy utterly," literally "pull up by the roots," from Latin eradicatus, past participle of eradicare "to root out, annihilate" (see eradication).

meanings.definitionCn: 发芽， 萌芽； 发展
垂头丧气的， 沮丧的

etymology.story: 1690s, from Latin despondentem (nominative despondens), present participle of despondere (see despondence).

meanings.definitionCn: 少许；琐事，小事；无价值的东西

morphology.mnemonic: 移动/迁移 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1690s, from Latin migratus, past participle of migrare "to move from one place to another" (see migration).

Old English Easterdæg, from Eastre (Northumbrian Eostre), from Proto-Germanic *austron-, "dawn," also the name of a goddess of fertility and spring, perhaps originally of sunrise, whose feast was celebrated at the spring equinox, from *aust- "east, toward the sunrise" (compare east), from PIE *aus- (1) "to shine" (especially of the dawn); see aurora.

meanings.definitionCn: 细绳，带子；一串；一系列；弦

etymology.story: Old English streng "line, cord, thread, string of a bow or harp," in plural "tackle, rigging; lineage, race," from Proto-Germanic *strangiz (source also of Old Norse strengr, Danish streng, Middle Dutch strenge, Dutch streng, Old High German strang, German Strang "rope, cord"), from *strang- "taut, stiff," from PIE root *strenk- "tight, narrow." Gradually restricted by early Middle English to lines that are smaller than a rope.

meanings.definitionCn: 提交， 呈递； 屈从； 主张， 建议

etymology.story: late 14c., "to place (oneself) under the control of another, to yield oneself," from Latin submittere "to yield, lower, let down, put under, reduce," from sub "under" (see sub-) + mittere "let go, send" (see mission).

meanings.definitionCn: 占用； 占据， 占领； 使忙于

etymology.story: mid-14c., "to take possession of," also "to take up space or time, employ (someone)," irregularly borrowed from Old French occuper "occupy (a person or place), hold, seize" (13c.) or directly from Latin occupare "take over, seize, take into possession, possess, occupy," from ob "over" (see ob-) + intensive form of capere "to grasp, seize" (see capable).

meanings.definitionCn: 进口， 输入； 意义， 要旨， 重要性

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + 流动 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: mid-15c., "flowing," from Middle French affluent (14c.) or directly from Latin affluentem (nominative affluens) "flowing toward, abounding, rich, copious," present participle of affluere "flow toward," from ad- "to" (see ad-) + fluere "to flow" (see fluent)..

morphology.mnemonic: 不/进入 + archaeological expedition + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., "difficulty or inability in digesting food," from Old French indigestion (13c.), from Late Latin indigestionem (nominative indigestio), from in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + digestionem "arrangement, distribution" (see digestion).

meanings.definitionCn: 侵入， 闯入； 打扰， 侵扰

etymology.story: early 15c., in an ecclesiastical sense, "take possession of (a prebend) not rightfully one's own," a back-formation from intrusion, or else from Latin intrudere "to thrust in, force in," from in- "in" (see in- (2)) + trudere "to thrust, push," from PIE *treud- "to press, push, squeeze" (see threat).

meanings.definitionCn: 简单， 容易； 朴素， 质朴

etymology.story: late 14c., "singleness of nature, unity, indivisibility; immutability," from Old French simplicite (12c., Modern French simplicité), from Latin simplicitatem (nominative simplicitas) "state of being simple, frankness, openness, artlessness, candor, directness," from simplex (genitive simplicis) "simple" (see simplex).
1590s, "a glance;" see blink (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 最高点， 峰顶， 极点； 峰会
使充满香气； 洒香水于
放大， 扩大； 夸大

etymology.story: late 14c., "to speak or act for the glory or honor (of someone or something)," from Old French magnefiier "glorify, magnify," from Latin magnificare "esteem greatly, extol, make much of," from magnificus "great, elevated, noble" (see magnificence).

1590s, from Latin redundantem (nominative redundans), present participle of redundare, literally "overflow, pour over; be over-full;" figuratively "be in excess," from re- "again" (see re-) + undare "rise in waves," from unda "a wave," from PIE *unda-, nasalized form of root *wed- (1) "water, wet" (see water (n.1)).
late 14c., from Old French durable (11c.), from Latin durabilis "lasting, permanent," from durare "to last, harden" (see endure).

meanings.definitionCn: 把…强加于； 征； 处以

etymology.story: late 14c., "to lay (a crime, duty, obligation, etc.) to the account of," from Old French imposer "put, place; impute, charge, accuse" (c.

1826, Englished form of Modern Latin Mammalia (1773), coined 1758 by Linnaeus for the class of mammals, from neuter plural of Late Latin mammalis "of the breast," from Latin mamma "breast," perhaps cognate with mamma..

meanings.definitionCn: 文字出现以前的， 没有文字的
要素， 因素； 因数； 系数

etymology.story: early 14c., from Latin ritus "religious observance or ceremony, custom, usage," perhaps from PIE root *re(i)- "to count, number" (source also of Greek arithmos "number," Old English rim "number;" see read (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 捏， 掐； 一撮， 微量

etymology.story: late 15c., "critical juncture" (as in baseball pinch hitter, attested from 1912), from pinch (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 恶作剧的， 顽皮的， 淘气的； 恶意的

etymology.story: early 14c., "unfortunate, disastrous," probably from mischief + -ous.

meanings.definitionCn: 战胜， 征服； 克服
苍白的， 无血色的； 憔悴的

etymology.story: Old English wann "dark, dusky, lacking luster," later "leaden, pale, gray," of uncertain origin, and not found in other Germanic languages.
early 15c., from Middle French continuité, from Latin continuitatem (nominative continuitas), from continuus (see continue).

meanings.definitionCn: 雕像， 雕塑品； 雕刻术

etymology.story: late 14c., from Latin sculptura "sculpture," from past participle stem of sculpere "to carve, engrave," back-formation from compounds such as exculpere, from scalpere "to carve, cut," from PIE root *(s)kel- (1) "to cut, cleave" (see scale (n.1))..
1610s, "action of making real," from realize + -ation.

"grow bigger or greater," Old English weaxan "to increase, grow" (class VII strong verb; past tense weox, past participle weaxen), from Proto-Germanic *wahsan (source also of Old Saxon, Old High German wahsan, Old Norse vaxa, Old Frisian waxa, Dutch wassen, German wachsen, Gothic wahsjan "to grow, increase"), from PIE *weg- (source also of Sanskrit vaksayati "cause to grow," Greek auxein "to increase"), extended form of root *aug- (1) "to increase" (see augment).
early 15c., from Latin contaminationem (nominative contaminatio), noun of action from past participle stem of contaminare (see contaminate).

early 15c., "suitable for plowing" (as opposed to pasture- or wood-land), from Old French arable (12c.), from Latin arabilis, from arare "to plow," from PIE *are- "to plow" (source also of Greek aroun, Old Church Slavonic orja, Lithuanian ariu "to plow;" Gothic arjan, Old English erian, Middle Irish airim, Welsh arddu "to plow;" Old Norse arþr "a plow").

meanings.definitionCn: 主旨， 要点， 大意

etymology.story: 1711, "the real point" (of a law case, etc.), from Anglo-French legalese phrases such as cest action gist "this action lies," from Old French gist en "it consists in, it lies in," from gist (Modern French gît), third person singular present indicative of gésir "to lie," from Latin iacet "it lies," from iacere "to lie, rest," related to iacere "to throw" (see jet (v.)).

"characteristic of city life, pertaining to cities or towns," 1610s (but rare before 1830s), from Latin urbanus "of or pertaining to a city or city life; in Rome," also "in city fashion, polished, refined, cultivated, courteous," but also sometimes "witty, facetious, bold, impudent;" as a noun, "city dweller," from urbs (genitive urbis) "city, walled town," a word of unknown origin.

meanings.definitionCn: 提到， 谈及； 查阅， 参考

etymology.story: late 14c., "to trace back (to a first cause), attribute, assign," from Old French referer (14c.) and directly from Latin referre "to relate, refer," literally "to carry back," from re- "back" (see re-) + ferre "carry" (see infer).

meanings.definitionCn: 星的， 恒星的， 星球的； 精彩的； 优秀的

etymology.story: 1650s, "pertaining to stars, star-like," from Late Latin stellaris "pertaining to a star, starry," from stella "star" (see star (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 火舌，火焰；光芒，光辉；强烈的感情

etymology.story: Middle English flaumen, also flaumben, flomben, flamben, flamen, flammen, c.
1590s, literally "to throw a card away," from dis- "away" + card (n.).

morphology.mnemonic: 沉降/补助 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: late 14c., from Anglo-French subsidie, Old French subside "help, aid, assistance, contribution," from Latin subsidium "a help, aid, assistance, (military) reinforcements, troops in reserve," from subsidere "to settle down, stay, remain" (see subside)..
1620s, plural of criterion (q.v.)..

meanings.definitionCn: 淫秽的， 下流的； 骇人听闻的； 可憎的， 可恶的

etymology.story: 1590s, "offensive to the senses, or to taste and refinement," from Middle French obscène (16c.), from Latin obscenus "offensive," especially to modesty, originally "boding ill, inauspicious," of unknown origin; perhaps from ob "onto" (see ob-) + caenum "filth." Meaning "offensive to modesty or decency" is attested from 1590s.

meanings.definitionCn: 用筏运送； 乘筏渡河
注意的， 专心的； 关心的

morphology.mnemonic: to pay attention + 有…倾向的

meanings.definitionCn: 禁止的， 抑制的； 高得令人难以承受的

morphology.mnemonic: 向前/支持 + 拥有/保持 + 有…倾向的

meanings.definitionCn: 才能， 本领； 天资

etymology.story: mid-14c., "an odor," from Old French flaire "odor or scent," especially in hunting, "fragrance, sense of smell," from flairier "to give off an odor; stink; smell sweetly" (Modern French flairer), from Vulgar Latin *flagrare, dissimilated from Latin fragrare "emit (a sweet) odor" (see fragrant).
1560s, "having moderate self-regard," from Middle French modeste (14c.), from Latin modestus "keeping due measure" (see modesty).

meanings.definitionCn: 碾碎； 使变形； 镇压

etymology.story: coined 1891 by Irish physicist George J.

meanings.definitionCn: 把…嵌入； 使深留脑中

etymology.story: 1778, "to lay in a bed (of surrounding matter)," from em- (1) + bed (n.).

1580s, "fortress commanding a city," from Middle French citadelle (15c.), from Italian cittadella, diminutive of Old Italian cittade "city" (Modern Italian citta), from Latin civitatem (nominative civitas; also source of Portuguese citadella, Spanish ciuadela; see city)..

meanings.definitionCn: 有希望的； 有前途的

etymology.story: "showing signs of future excellence," c.

meanings.definitionCn: 对立的， 感情矛盾的

morphology.mnemonic: ambi + 强壮/价值 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: 1916, originally a term in psychology; back-formation from ambivalence.

meanings.definitionCn: 能实行的， 可行的， 适用的

etymology.story: 1670s, from Middle French pratiquable (1590s), from pratiquer "to practice," from Medieval Latin practicare "to practice" (see practical).

1560s, "study and description of natural objects," from Middle French physiologie or directly from Latin physiologia "natural science, study of nature," from Greek physiologia "natural science, inquiry into nature," from physio- "nature" (see physio-) + logia "study" (see -logy).

meanings.definitionCn: 高耸的； 崇高的； 高傲的

etymology.story: "exalted, of high rank," early 15c.; also "with a high purpose," from loft + -y (2).

1610s, "open to the understanding, not obscure or ambiguous," from French explicite, from Latin explicitus "unobstructed," variant past participle of explicare "unfold, unravel, explain," from ex- "out" (see ex-) + plicare "to fold" (see ply (v.1)).
1794, from French utiliser, from Italian utilizzare, from utile "usable," from Latin utilis "usable," from uti "make use of, profit by, take advantage of" (see use (v.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 盛行， 流行； 战胜， 压倒； 劝说

morphology.mnemonic: 在…之前/预先 + 强壮/价值

etymology.story: mid-15c., "feed on buds," from Middle French brouster, from Old French broster "to sprout, bud," from brost "young shoot, twig," probably from Proto-Germanic *brust- "bud, shoot," from PIE *bhreus- "to swell, sprout" (see breast (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 含蓄的； 绝对的； 内含的

etymology.story: 1590s, "implied, resting on inference," from Middle French implicite and directly from Latin implicitus, later variant of implicatus "entangled, confused, involved," past participle of implicare "entangle, involve" (see implication).

meanings.definitionCn: 纯度； 纯洁， 纯净， 纯粹

etymology.story: "to divide in portions," early 14c., from Old French porcioner "share out, divide in portions," from porcion (see portion (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 无效的， 无用的， 无意义的； 琐细的； 没出息的

etymology.story: "incapable of producing result," 1550s, from Middle French futile or directly from Latin futilis, futtilis "vain, worthless, futile," a figurative use, literally "pouring out easily, easily emptied" (the Latin adjective used as a noun meant "a water vessel broad above and pointed below"), hence "leaky, unreliable," from fundere "to pour, melt," from PIE root *gheu- "to pour" (see found (v.2)).
late 14c., from Old French pensif "thoughtful, distracted, musing" (11c.), from penser "to think," from Latin pensare "weigh, consider," frequentative of pendere "weigh" (see pendant).
late 15c., agent noun from relieve.
1530s, from Latin acumen "a point, sting," hence "mental sharpness, shrewdness," from acuere "to sharpen" (see acuity)..

1540s, "calamitous, disastrous, fatal" ("resembling the actions in a stage tragedy"), shortened from tragical (late 15c.), modeled on Latin tragicus, from Greek tragikos "of or pertaining to tragedy; stately, majestic; plaintive," literally "goatish, of or pertaining to a goat," and perhaps referring to a satyr impersonated by a goat singer or satyric actor, from tragodia (see tragedy).

meanings.definitionCn: 避开， 戒除， 回避

etymology.story: mid-14c., from Old French eschiver "shun, eschew, avoid, dispense with," from Frankish *skiuhan "dread, avoid, shun," from Proto-Germanic *skeukhwaz (source also of Old High German sciuhen "to avoid, escape," German scheuen "to fear, shun, shrink from," scheu "shy, timid"); see shy (adj.).

early 13c., fisicien "a healer, a medical practitioner," from Old French fisiciien "physician, doctor, sage" (12c., Modern French physicien means "physicist"), from fisique "art of healing," from Latin physica "natural science" (see physic).
1860 in reference to celebrity, from star (n.) + -dom.
late 15c., "to heap up for oneself," from Old French amasser, from à "to" (see ad-) + masser (see mass (n.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: 敢于冒险的； 有危险的

morphology.mnemonic: enterprise of a business nature + remarkable

etymology.story: 1660s, from venture + -some (1)..

1775, "a remembrance or memory," from French souvenir (12c.), from Old French noun use of souvenir (v.) "to remember, come to mind," from Latin subvenire "come to mind," from sub- "up from below" (see sub-) + venire "to come" (see venue).

meanings.definitionCn: 古色古香的； 离奇有趣的
控制；紧握；吸引住…的注意
控制， 影响力； 紧握； 理解

etymology.story: 1550s, ternado, navigator's word for violent windy thunderstorm in the tropical Atlantic, probably a mangled borrowing from Spanish tronada "thunderstorm," from tronar "to thunder," from Latin tonare "to thunder" (see thunder (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 探索， 勘探； 探险； 探究

etymology.story: 1580s, "to investigate, examine," a back-formation from exploration, or else from Middle French explorer (16c.), from Latin explorare "investigate, search out, examine, explore," said to be originally a hunters' term meaning "set up a loud cry," from ex- "out" (see ex-) + plorare "to weep, cry."
word formed from the first letters of a series of words, 1943, American English coinage from acro- + -onym "name" (abstracted from homonym; see name (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 显著的， 值得注目的

morphology.mnemonic: notice, attention, reputation + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: 1550s, from note (v.) + worthy.
1630s, "to practice fraud or trickery," also a noun (1630s, now obsolete) "a needy, disreputable parasite" [OED], perhaps from German schurke "scoundrel, rogue, knave, villain" (see shark (n.)).
1640s (Browne, from Gilbert's Modern Latin), from electric (q.v.) + -ity.

meanings.definitionCn: 试验性的； 不确定的， 暂定的； 犹豫的

etymology.story: 1580s, from Medieval Latin tentativus "trying, testing," from Latin tentatus, past participle of tentare "to feel, try."

meanings.definitionCn: 气候； 气候区， 地带； 风气， 氛围； 思潮

etymology.story: late 14c., "horizontal zone of the earth," Scottish, from Old French climat "region, part of the earth," from Latin clima (genitive climatis) "region; slope of the Earth," from Greek klima "region, zone," literally "an inclination, slope," thus "slope of the Earth from equator to pole," from root of klinein "to slope, to lean" (see lean (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 巨大的； 势不可挡的； 惊人的， 令人吃惊的

etymology.story: "amazing," 1560s, figurative present participle adjective from stagger (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 地壳； 外壳； 硬的表面； 面包片

etymology.story: late 14c.; see crust (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 批评； 吹毛求疵， 非难； 评论

root.meaning: one who judges merits of books, plays, etc.

morphology.mnemonic: one who judges merits of books, plays, etc. + ize

etymology.story: 1640s, "to pass judgment on something" (usually unfavorable), from critic + -ize.
"unbelieving," 1570s, from Latin incredulus "unbelieving, incredulous," from in- "not" (see in- (1)) + credulus (see credulous).
late 15c., "to sorrow," from Late Latin condolere "to suffer with another," from com- "with" (see com-) + dolere "to grieve" (see doleful).

meanings.definitionCn: 格子， 栅格； 地图上的坐标方格， 网格； 输电网， 煤气输送网

etymology.story: 1839, shortening of gridiron or griddle.

meanings.definitionCn: 周围， 周边； 圆周； 周长

etymology.story: late 14c., from Latin circumferentia, neuter plural of circumferens, present participle of circumferre "to lead around, take around, carry around," from circum "around" (see circum-) + ferre "to carry" (see infer).

meanings.definitionCn: 猛烈地继续，激烈进行；发怒，怒斥；迅速蔓延，猖獗
风靡一时的事物， 时尚； 狂怒
使完成； 把…最后定下来； 定稿
担任主席； 主持； 掌管

etymology.story: 1610s, from French présider "preside over, govern" (15c.), from Latin praesidere "stand guard; superintend," literally "sit in front of," from prae "before" (see pre-) + sedere "to sit" (see sedentary)..

meanings.definitionCn: 登机， 上船； 搭伙， 寄宿
膳食费用；委员会，董事会；板，木板

etymology.story: verb senses derived from various senses of board (n.1) and board (n.2) include "come alongside" (a ship), mid-15c.

meanings.definitionCn: 经济的； 经济上的； 经济学的； 合算的

etymology.story: 1590s, "pertaining to management of a household," perhaps shortened from economical, or else from French économique or directly from Latin oeconomicus "of domestic economy," from Greek oikonomikos "practiced in the management of a household or family" (also the name of a treatise by Xenophon on the duties of domestic life), hence, "frugal, thrifty," from oikonomia "household management" (see economy (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 填满， 塞满； 让…吃饱

etymology.story: early 14c., "quilted material worn under chain mail," from Old French estoffe "quilted material, furniture, provisions" (Modern French étoffe), from estoffer "to equip or stock," which according to French sources is from Old High German stopfon "to plug, stuff," or from a related Frankish word (see stop (v.)), but OED has "strong objections" to this.

root.meaning: to cause to work or open,

morphology.mnemonic: off + to cause to work or open,

etymology.story: Old English ofspring "children or young collectively, descendants," literally "those who spring off (someone,)" from off + springan "to spring" (see spring (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 一时的兴致， 冲动； 怪念头， 奇想

etymology.story: 1640s, "play on words, pun," shortened from whimwham "fanciful object" (q.v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 逮捕， 拘留； 停止， 阻止； 吸引

etymology.story: late 14c., from Anglo-French arest, Old French areste (n.), from arester "to stay, stop" (see arrest (v.))..

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + sort + 结果/过程/状态

etymology.story: 1610s, "action of assorting," from assort + -ment.

meanings.definitionCn: 部分； 地区； 部门； 段落， 章节； 截面， 剖面

morphology.mnemonic: cut切割 + 状态/行为/过程

meanings.definitionCn: 溢出； 容纳不下的物

morphology.mnemonic: 过度/超越 + amount that flows

meanings.definitionCn: 挑动， 激怒， 挑衅； 引发， 引起

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French provoker, provochier (12c., Modern French provoquer) and directly from Latin provocare "call forth, challenge," from pro- "forth" (see pro-) + vocare "to call" (see voice (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 另样，用别的方法；在其他方面

etymology.story: contracted from Old English phrase on oðre wisan "in the other manner" (see other + wise (n.)), which in Middle English became oþre wise, and mid-14c.

meanings.definitionCn: 显著的； 有记号的； 被监视的

etymology.story: "having a mark," Old English gemearcodan (see mark (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 病态的； 病理学的； 不理智的， 无道理的

morphology.mnemonic: 感情/疾病 + ic + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1680s, "pertaining to disease," formed in English from pathologic + -al (1).

meanings.definitionCn: 音乐演奏会； 诗歌朗诵会； 赘述

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + 召唤/引用 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1510s, a legal term, "rehearsal or statement of relevant facts," from recite + -al.
mid-14c., from Old French visable, visible "perceptible" (12c.) and directly from Latin visibilis "that may be seen," from visus, past participle of videre "to see" (see vision).

meanings.definitionCn: 使适应； 确定方向， 定位
往复的， 反复的， 再次发生的

etymology.story: 1711, present participle adjective from recur..
mid-15c., a back-formation from tranquility or else from Latin tranquillus "quiet, calm, still."

meanings.definitionCn: 令人高兴的， 愉快的

etymology.story: late 15c., from Old French voyager, from voiage (see voyage (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 灵敏的， 敏锐的； 热心的， 渴望的； 激烈的； 锋利的

etymology.story: "lament loudly over the dead, bitterly wail," 1811, from Irish caoinim "I weep, wail, lament," from Old Irish coinim "I wail." Hence "to utter in a shrill voice" (1893).

morphology.mnemonic: 营养 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: early 15c., from Old French nutrition (14c.) and directly from Latin nutritionem (nominative nutritio) "a nourishing," noun of action from past participle stem of nutrire "to nourish, suckle" (see nourish)..

morphology.mnemonic: 统治 + ee + 主义/学说/运动

etymology.story: 1829, from absentee + -ism; originally in reference to landlords, especially in Ireland (absentee in this sense is in Johnson's dictionary); reference to pupils or workers is from 1922..
1832, "grass yielding edible grain," originally an adjective (1818) "having to do with edible grain," from French céréale (16c., "of Ceres;" 18c.

meanings.definitionCn: 仔细检查； 彻底检修
仔细检查； 彻底检修
初级的， 基础的； 基本的； 简单的

etymology.story: late 14c., "having the nature of one of the four elements," from Middle French elementaire and directly from Latin elementarius "belonging to the elements or rudiments," from elementum (see element).

meanings.definitionCn: 庄严的， 严肃的； 隆重的

etymology.story: mid-14c., "performed with due religious ceremony or reverence, sacred, devoted to religious observances," also, of a vow, etc., "made under religious sanction, binding," from Old French solempne (12c., Modern French solennel) and directly from Latin sollemnis "annual, established, religiously fixed, formal, ceremonial, traditional," perhaps related to sollus "whole" (see safe (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 感知， 察觉； 理解
政治； 政纲， 政见； 政治事务； 权术； 政治学

etymology.story: 1520s, "science of government," from politic (adj.), modeled on Aristotle's ta politika "affairs of state," the name of his book on governing and governments, which was in English mid-15c.

meanings.definitionCn: 严重的； 极度的， 激烈的； 敏锐的

etymology.story: late 14c., originally of fevers and diseases, "coming and going quickly" (opposed to a chronic), from Latin acutus "sharp, pointed," figuratively "shrill, penetrating; intelligent, cunning," past participle of acuere "sharpen" (see acuity).

meanings.definitionCn: 污点， 耻辱； 烙印； 特征

etymology.story: 1590s (earlier stigme, c.

meanings.definitionCn: 反常， 变态； 病理学； 病状

morphology.mnemonic: 感情/疾病 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: "science of diseases," 1610s, from French pathologie (16c.), from medical Latin pathologia "study of disease," from Greek pathos "suffering" (see pathos) + -logia "study" (see -logy).

meanings.definitionCn: 硅石， 硅土； 二氧化硅

etymology.story: "hard silicon dioxide," 1801, Modern Latin, from Latin silex (genitive silicis) "flint, pebble," on model of alumina, soda..

meanings.definitionCn: 至关重要的， 决定性的

etymology.story: 1706, "cross-shaped," from French crucial, a medical term for ligaments of the knee (which cross each other), from Latin crux (genitive crucis) "cross" (see cross (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 贡献， 捐赠； 有助于； 投稿

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 给予/分配

etymology.story: 1520s, from Latin contributus, past participle of contribuere "to bring together, add, unite, collect, contribute" (see contribution).

meanings.definitionCn: 评论， 评语； 注释

etymology.story: 1650s, from Modern Latin microscopium, literally "an instrument for viewing what is small," from Greek micro- (see micro-) + -skopion, from skopein "to look, see" (see -scope)..

meanings.definitionCn: 奋斗， 努力； 力求

morphology.mnemonic: 发光/闪耀 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1520s, "a mushroom," from Latin fungus "a mushroom, fungus;" used in English at first as a learned alternative to mushroom (funge was used in this sense late 14c.).

meanings.definitionCn: 聪明的， 理智的； 易懂的， 易理解的

etymology.story: late 14c., "able to understand, intelligent," from Latin intelligibilis, intellegibilis "that can understand; that can be understood," from intellegere "to understand, come to know" (see intelligence).

Old English andlang "entire, continuous; extended; all day long; alongside of," from and- "opposite, against" (from Proto-Germanic *andi-, *anda-, from PIE *anti "against," locative singular of *ant- "front, forehead;" see ante) + lang "long" (see long (adj.)).

"dismiss," 1590s, from Middle Dutch casseren, kaseeren "to cast off, discharge," from French casser "to discharge, annul," from Late Latin cassare "annul," from Latin cassus "void, empty" (see caste (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 无活动的， 惰性的； 不活泼的， 迟钝的

etymology.story: 1640s, "without inherent force, having no power to act or respond," from French inerte (16c.) or directly from Latin inertem (nominative iners) "unskilled, incompetent; inactive, helpless, weak, sluggish; worthless," used of stagnant fluids, uncultivated pastures, expressionless eyes.
late 14c., "to regulate one's diet for the sake of health," from Old French dieter, from diete (see diet (n.1)); meaning "to regulate oneself as to food" (especially against fatness) is from 1650s.

meanings.definitionCn: 掩盖， 掩饰， 假装

morphology.mnemonic: 分开/二 + 相似/相同 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1530s, from Latin dissimulatus, past participle of dissimulare "to disguise, hide, conceal, keep secret," from dis- (see dis-) + simulare (see simulate).

1881, as a biochemical term, from German Enzym, coined 1878 by German physiologist Wilhelm Kühne (1837-1900), from Modern Greek enzymos "leavened," from en "in" (see en- (2)) + zyme "leaven" (see zymurgy).

1889, socca, later socker (1891), soccer (1895), originally university slang (with jocular formation -er (3)), from a shortened form of Assoc., abbreviation of association in Football Association (as opposed to Rugby football);
Old English buterian "spread butter on," from the same source as butter (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 智力， 才智； 风趣； 机智幽默的人

etymology.story: "to know" (archaic), Old English witan (past tense wast, past participle witen) "to know, beware of or conscious of, understand, observe, ascertain, learn," from Proto-Germanic *witan "to have seen," hence "to know" (source also of Old Saxon witan, Old Norse vita, Old Frisian wita, Middle Dutch, Dutch weten, Old High German wizzan, German wissen, Gothic witan "to know"), from PIE *weid- (see wit (n.)).
late 14c., from Old French respirer (12c.), from Latin respirare "breathe again, breathe in and out," from re- "again" (see re-) + spirare "to breathe" (see spirit (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 选择的， 选择性的； 精挑细选的

etymology.story: 1620s; see select (adj.) + -ive.

morphology.mnemonic: to pass (something to someone) + 充满…的

etymology.story: Old English handful "as much as can be held in the open hand;" see hand (n.) + -ful.

meanings.definitionCn: 回扣， 折扣； 退还款

etymology.story: plural stimuli, 1680s, originally as a medical term, "something that goads a lazy organ" (often the male member), from a modern use of Latin stimulus "a goad, a pointed stick," figuratively "a sting, a pang; incitement, spur," from PIE *sti- "point, prick, pierce" (see stick (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 实物交易， 以物易物

etymology.story: mid-15c., apparently from Old French barater "to barter, cheat, deceive, haggle" (also, "to have sexual intercourse"), 12c., which is of uncertain origin, perhaps from a Celtic language (compare Irish brath "treachery").

meanings.definitionCn: 使迷惑， 使不知所措

etymology.story: 1680s, from be- "thoroughly" + archaic wilder "lead astray, lure into the wilds," probably a back-formation from wilderness.

morphology.mnemonic: 终止/界限 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: "mixture of liquid hydrocarbons used as an illuminating or heating fluid," 1852, from Greek keros "wax" (see cere) + chemical suffix -ene.

meanings.definitionCn: 降低， 贬低； 减少， 减弱

etymology.story: early 15c., from merger of two obsolete verbs, diminue and minish.

meanings.definitionCn: 超过， 超越， 胜过

etymology.story: 1550s, from Middle French surpasser "go beyond, exceed, excel" (16c.), from sur- "beyond" (see sur- (1)) + passer "to go by" (see pass (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 语言学家； 通晓数国语言的人

etymology.story: 1580s, "a master of language, one who uses his tongue freely," a hybrid from Latin lingua "language, tongue" (see lingual) + -ist.
early 14c., from Old French oistre (Modern French huître), from Latin ostrea, plural or fem.

meanings.definitionCn: 远离的； 冷淡的， 冷漠的

etymology.story: 1530s, from a- (1) + Middle English loof "weather gage," also "windward direction," probably from Dutch loef (Middle Dutch lof) "the weather side of a ship." Originally a nautical order to keep the ship's head to the wind, thus to stay clear of a lee-shore or some other quarter; hence the figurative sense of "at a distance, apart" (1580s).

meanings.definitionCn: 注意， 留心； 立正

morphology.mnemonic: to pay attention + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., "giving heed," from Latin attentionem (nominative attentio) "attention, attentiveness," noun of action from past participle stem of attendere "mental heeding" (see attend).

meanings.definitionCn: 屈服， 屈从； 死亡

etymology.story: late 15c., from Old French succomber "succumb, die, lose one's (legal) case," and directly from Latin succumbere "submit, surrender, yield, be overcome; sink down; lie under; cohabit with," from sub "down" (see sub-) + -cumbere "take a reclining position," related to cubare "lie down" (see cubicle).

morphology.mnemonic: 不/分离 + 向前/支持 + portion + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1550s, from dis- "not" + proportionate.

morphology.mnemonic: 出/向外/前任 + 伸展/趋向

etymology.story: early 14c., from Anglo-French extente, estente "extent, extension;" in law, "valuation of land, stretch of land," from fem.
1590s, from Latin translucentem (nominative translucens), present participle of translucere "to shine through," from trans- "through" (see trans-) + lucere "to shine" (see light (n.)).
mid-15c., "dregs," from Latin magma "dregs of an ointment," from Greek magma "thick unguent, ointment," from root of  massein "to knead, mold," from PIE *mag- "to knead" (see macerate).

Old English finn "fin," from Proto-Germanic *finno (source also of Middle Low German vinne, Dutch vin), perhaps from Latin pinna "feather, wing" (see pin (n.)); or, less likely, from Latin spina "thorn, spine" (see spine).

meanings.definitionCn: 修理；使固定，安装；安排；找到，确定
解决方法； 困境， 窘境

etymology.story: "position from which it is difficult to move," 1809, American English, from fix (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 殖民的； 殖民国家的

morphology.mnemonic: 殖民/定居 + 具有…特征/名词化 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1756, from Latin colonia (see colony) + -al (1), or directly from colony on model of baronoinal.

meanings.definitionCn: 巨大的动物； 才智超群的人
栖息，停留；把…置于较高处；坐于
栖息处， 栖木； 高处， 较高的位置； 鲈鱼

etymology.story: "to roost," late 14c., from Old French perchier "to sit on a perch" (of a bird), from perche (n.) (see perch (n.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: 着火的， 燃烧的； 闪耀的； 情绪激动的

etymology.story: late 14c., from a "on" (see a- (1)) + blaze (n.)..
"complain," 1885 (implied in agent noun grouser), British Army slang, of uncertain origin.

meanings.definitionCn: 加， 加法； 添加， 增加

morphology.mnemonic: to put, place, + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., "action of adding numbers;" c.

meanings.definitionCn: 讨价过高，索价过高；过量装填；渲染，夸张
无礼的； 俏皮的； 漂亮的
忽略， 忽视； 疏忽

etymology.story: 1640s, from cosmo- + -ic.

meanings.definitionCn: 静脉； 叶脉； 纹理， 纹路； 方式， 风格

etymology.story: Old English ceorfan (class III strong verb; past tense cearf, past participle corfen) "to cut, cut down, slay; to carve, cut out, engrave," from West Germanic *kerfan (source also of Old Frisian kerva, Middle Dutch and Dutch kerven, German kerben "to cut, notch"), from PIE root *gerbh- "to scratch," making carve the English cognate of Greek graphein "to write," originally "to scratch" on clay tablets with a stylus.

meanings.definitionCn: 收缩， 缩小； 退缩， 畏缩

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French veler, voiller (12c.), from Latin velare "to cover, veil," from velum "a cloth, covering, curtain, veil," literally "a sail" (see veil (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 祖先， 祖宗； 原种； 原型
统治集团； 等级制度； 阶层

etymology.story: late 14c., jerarchie, ierarchie, "rank in the sacred order; one of the three divisions of the nine orders of angels;" loosely, "rule, dominion," from Old French ierarchie (14c., Modern French hiérarchie), from Medieval Latin hierarchia "ranked division of angels" (in the system of Dionysius the Areopagite), from Greek hierarkhia "rule of a high priest," from hierarkhes "high priest, leader of sacred rites," from ta hiera "the sacred rites" (neuter plural of hieros "sacred;" see ire) + arkhein "to lead, rule" (see archon).

meanings.definitionCn: 联盟， 同盟； 等级， 级别

etymology.story: 1620s, from Latin textilis "a web, canvas, woven fabric, cloth, something woven," noun use of textilis "woven, wrought," from texere "to weave," from PIE root *teks- "to make" (see texture (n.)).
1620s, "fact of having a place," from French localité, from Late Latin localitatem (nominative localitas) "locality," from localis "belonging to a place" (see local).

meanings.definitionCn: 神秘的； 易乎寻常的

etymology.story: 1590s, "mischievous;" 1773 in the sense of "associated with the supernatural," originally Scottish and northern English, from un- (1) "not" + canny..
"one who gives form," mid-14c., agent noun from form (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 延长， 延伸； 扩大…的范围； 舒展

morphology.mnemonic: 出/向外/前任 + 伸展/趋向

etymology.story: early 14c., "to value, assess," from Anglo-French estendre (late 13c.), Old French estendre "stretch out, extend, increase," transitive and intransitive (Modern French étendre), from Latin extendere "stretch out, spread out; increase, enlarge, prolong, continue," from ex- "out" (see ex-) + tendere "to stretch" (see tenet).

meanings.definitionCn: 褶，褶层；褶皱；褶痕，褶缝；羊栏，

etymology.story: Old English faldan (Mercian), fealdan (West Saxon), transitive, "to bend (cloth) back over itself, wrap up, furl," class VII strong verb (past tense feold, past participle fealden), from Proto-Germanic *falthan, *faldan (source also of Middle Dutch vouden, Dutch vouwen, Old Norse falda, Middle Low German volden, Old High German faldan, German falten, Gothic falþan).

"narrow valley between cliffs," 1834, from Mexican Spanish cañon, extended sense of Spanish cañon "a pipe, tube; deep hollow, gorge," augmentative of cano "a tube," from Latin canna "reed" (see cane (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 不能穿透的； 不可理解的， 高深莫测的

etymology.story: mid-15c., from Middle French impenetrable (14c.) or directly from Latin impenetrabilis "that cannot be penetrated," from assimilated form of in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + penetrabilis "penetrable" (see penetrable).

meanings.definitionCn: 考虑， 思考； 体谅， 照顾； 考虑因素， 理由； 报酬

morphology.mnemonic: stretch, extend, + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: mid-14c., "a beholding, looking at," also "keeping in mind," from Old French consideracion (12c., Modern French considération), from Latin considerationem (nominative consideratio) "consideration, contemplation, reflection," noun of action from past participle stem of considerare (see consider).

"to free a letter for carriage or an article for publication, to send by public conveyance free of expense," 1708, from shortened form of French affranchir, from a- "to" + franchir "to free" (see franchise (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 写、 题、 铭刻； 铭记

etymology.story: 1550s, "to write on or in" (something durable and conspicuous), from Latin inscribere "to write in or on," (see inscription).

meanings.definitionCn: 钦佩， 仰慕； 欣赏

etymology.story: late 14c., "venomous substance," from Latin virus "poison, sap of plants, slimy liquid, a potent juice," probably from PIE root *weis- "to melt away, to flow," used of foul or malodorous fluids, with specialization in some languages to "poisonous fluid" (source also of Sanskrit visam "poison," visah "poisonous;" Avestan vish- "poison;" Latin viscum "sticky substance, birdlime;" Greek ios "poison," ixos "mistletoe, birdlime;" Old Church Slavonic višnja "cherry;" Old Irish fi "poison;" Welsh gwy "fluid, water," gwyar "blood").

late 15c., from Middle French metaphore (Old French metafore, 13c.), and directly from Latin metaphora, from Greek metaphora "a transfer," especially of the sense of one word to a different word, literally "a carrying over," from metapherein "transfer, carry over; change, alter; to use a word in a strange sense," from meta- "over, across" (see meta-) + pherein "to carry, bear" (see infer)..
1770, "particular style of art," a French word in English (nativized from c.
early 15c., from Middle French permanence and directly from Medieval Latin permanentia (early 14c.), from Latin permanens (see permanent).

meanings.definitionCn: 面板，镶板；仪表盘；专门小组
人类； 人性； 人道， 仁慈

etymology.story: late 14c., "kindness, graciousness, politeness; consideration for others," from Old French humanité, umanité "human nature; humankind, life on earth; pity," from Latin humanitatem (nominative humanitas) "human nature; the human race, mankind;" also "humane conduct, philanthropy, kindness; good breeding, refinement," from humanus (see human (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 优秀的， 杰出的， 出色的； 未解决的； 未偿付的

morphology.mnemonic: 超过/外部 + place of standing, position

etymology.story: 1610s, "projecting, prominent, detached," present participle adjective from outstand (v.) "endure successfully, hold out against," from out (adv.) + stand (v.).
1739, American English, from Spanish adobe, from oral form of Arabic al-tob "the brick," from Coptic tube "brick," a word found in hieroglyphics..

meanings.definitionCn: 交替的， 轮流的； 间隔的
不能架桥的； 不能逾越的

etymology.story: 1660s, from Italian infuriato, from Medieval Latin infuriatus, past participle of infuriare "to madden, enrage," from Latin in furia "in a fury," from ablative of furia (see fury).
1839, flue, shortening of influenza.

meanings.definitionCn: 跳水； 猛跌， 骤降； 卷入

morphology.mnemonic: inquiring, reflective + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: also sceptical, 1630s; see skeptic + -al (1).

morphology.mnemonic: 向下/移除/取消 + to write in code

etymology.story: 1520s, from de- + cipher (v.).
early 14c., "grow, sprout, blossom," from Anglo-French burjuner, Old French borjoner "to bud, sprout," from borjon "a bud, shoot, pimple" (Modern French bourgeon), of uncertain origin.

meanings.definitionCn: 天真的， 率真的， 质朴的； 幼稚的， 无知的， 轻信的； 稚拙派的

etymology.story: 1650s, "natural, simple, artless," from French naïve, fem.
1818, from sew (v.) "to drain, draw off water" (late 15c., from sewer (n.1)) + -age..

meanings.definitionCn: 基本的，本质的；极其重要的，必不可少的
要素， 实质； 必需品

root.meaning: ingredient which gives something its particular character

morphology.mnemonic: ingredient which gives something its particular character + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: mid-14c., "that is such by its essence," from Late Latin essentialis, from essentia "essence" (see essence).

morphology.mnemonic: 出/向外/前任 + 悬挂/称量/支付 + 行为/过程/结果

etymology.story: 1769, "act of expending," from Medieval Latin expenditus, irregular past participle of Latin expendere "to weigh out; to pay out" (see expend) + -ure.

early 14c., "make illustrious, make known," from Old French clarifiier "clarify, make clear, explain" (12c.), from Late Latin clarificare "to make clear," also "to glorify," from Latin clarificus "brilliant," from clarus "clear, distinct" (see clear (adj.)) + root of facere "to make, do" (see factitious).

meanings.definitionCn: 火炉， 熔炉； 锅炉

etymology.story: early 13c., from Old French fornais "oven, furnace," figuratively "flame of love" (12c.), from Latin fornacem (nominative fornax) "an oven, kiln," related to fornus/furnus "oven," and to formus "warm," from PIE root *gwher- "warm" (source also of Greek thermos, Old English wearm; see warm (adj.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 出没于；萦绕在心头；长期缠扰
围巾， 披巾， 头巾

etymology.story: "eat hastily," 1960, U.S.

meanings.definitionCn: 情投意合的； 相宜的， 适宜的； 适当的， 合适的

etymology.story: 1620s, "kindred, sympathetic," from Latin com- "together" (see com-) + genialis "of birth," thus, "kindred" (see genus).

meanings.definitionCn: 图形， 图像； 制图法， 制图学

etymology.story: by 1842 as "the art of precise mechanical drawing;" by 1889 in reference to the use of diagrams, from graphic; also see -ics.

meanings.definitionCn: 遵守， 奉行； 观察； 察觉， 看到； 评说， 评论

etymology.story: late 14c., "to hold to" (a manner of life or course of conduct), from Old French observer, osserver "to observe, watch over, follow" (10c.), from Latin observare "watch over, note, heed, look to, attend to, guard, regard, comply with," from ob "over" (see ob-) + servare "to watch, keep safe," from PIE root *ser- (1) "to protect." Meaning "to attend to in practice, to keep, follow" is attested from late 14c.

meanings.definitionCn: 意味深长的； 有目的的， 有用意的； 有意义的

etymology.story: 1827, from meaning (n.) + -ful.

meanings.definitionCn: 表演， 演唱； 提供， 给予； 翻译
挖掘， 发掘； 出土文物； 挖掘现场

etymology.story: 1610s, "action of excavating," from Latin excavationem (nominative excavatio) "a hollowing out," noun of action from past participle stem of excavare "to hollow out" (see excavate).

meanings.definitionCn: 稀有的， 罕见的； 半熟的

etymology.story: "rise up," 1833, dialectal variant of rear (v.1).

meanings.definitionCn: 融合， 合并； 联合， 结合

morphology.mnemonic: 统治 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1610s, noun of action from archaic amalgam (v.) "to alloy with mercury" (see amalgamate).
1859, from creative + -ity.

morphology.mnemonic: 使成为/放入 + something used for igniting + 使变成/变得

etymology.story: late 14c., "to remove the dimness or blindness" (usually figurative, from one's eyes or heart); see en- (1) + lighten.

root.meaning: to be under obligation (to do something)

morphology.mnemonic: to be under obligation (to do something) + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: late 12c., neodi "poor, indigent," from need (n.) + adjectival suffix -y (2).
Old English utmest (Anglian) "outermost," double superlative of ut "out" (see out (adv.)) + -most.

meanings.definitionCn: 形象， 印象； 画像； 影像， 映像； 意象， 比喻

etymology.story: late 14c., "to form a mental picture (of something), imagine," from Old French imagier, from image (see image (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 狼吞虎咽的， 贪婪的； 求知欲强的

etymology.story: 1630s, formed as an adjectival form of voracity.

meanings.definitionCn: 不可预知的； 无法预言的

morphology.mnemonic: 不/非/否定 + 在…之前/预先 + 说/讲 + able

etymology.story: 1840, from un- (1) "not" + predictable (adj.).

meanings.definitionCn: 地形学； 地形， 地貌

etymology.story: early 15c., "description of a place," from Late Latin topographia, from Greek topographia "a description of a place," from topos "place" (see topos) + -graphia (see -graphy).

meanings.definitionCn: 带状物； 丝带， 缎带； 绶带； 色带

etymology.story: early 14c., ribane, from Old French riban "a ribbon," variant of ruban (13c.), of unknown origin, possibly from a Germanic compound whose second element is related to band (n.1); compare Middle Dutch ringhband "necklace." Modern spelling is from mid-16c.

meanings.definitionCn: 描绘； 扮演， 饰演

etymology.story: mid-13c., "to draw, paint" (something), from Anglo-French purtraire, Old French portraire "to draw, to paint, portray" (12c.), literally "trace, draw forth," from por- "forth" (from Latin pro-; see pro-) + traire "trace, draw," from Latin trahere "to drag, draw" (see tract (n.1)).

late 14c., "state of extreme dread or suspense," also "a half-conscious or insensible condition, state of insensibility to mundane things," from Old French transe "fear of coming evil," originally "coma, passage from life to death" (12c.), from transir "be numb with fear," originally "die, pass on," from Latin transire "cross over" (see transient).
"rub hard," early 15c., earlier shrubben (c.

meanings.definitionCn: 巨大的， 深远的； 知识渊博的； 深奥的
断言， 坚持声称； 证实， 确认
同类的， 相似的， 同质的

etymology.story: 1640s, from Medieval Latin homogeneus, from Greek homogenes "of the same kind," from homos "same" (see homo- (1)) + genos "kind, gender, race, stock" (see genus).

meanings.definitionCn: 必需品； 必要， 需要； 必然性

etymology.story: late 14c., "constraining power of circumstances," from Old French necessité "need, necessity; privation, poverty; distress, torment; obligation, duty" (12c.), from Latin necessitatem (nominative necessitas) "compulsion, need for attention, unavoidableness, destiny," from necesse (see necessary).

meanings.definitionCn: 设计， 构成； 给…镶框； 表达
营业性的厅； 沙龙； 客厅， 会客厅

etymology.story: 1690s, "large room or apartment in a palace or great house," from French salon "reception room" (17c.), from Italian salone "large hall," from sala "hall," from a Germanic source (compare Old English sele, Old Norse salr "hall," Old High German sal "hall, house," German Saal), from Proto-Germanic *salaz, from PIE *sel- (1) "human settlement" (source also of Old Church Slavonic selo "courtyard, village," obsolete Polish siolo, Russian selo "village," Lithuanian sala "village").

meanings.definitionCn: 声音的，嗓音的，发声的；直言不讳的

morphology.mnemonic: 呼叫/声音 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: late 14c., "spoken, oral," from Old French vocal (13c.), from Latin vocalis "sounding, sonorous, speaking," as a noun, "a vowel," from vox (genitive vocis) "voice" (see voice (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 耕田， 犁田， 耕作
握紧； 抱紧； 扣住， 扣牢
徘徊， 彷徨； 翱翔， 盘旋

etymology.story: "having no definite aim or purpose," 1650s, from at random (1560s), "at great speed" (thus, "carelessly, haphazardly"), alteration of Middle English noun randon "impetuosity, speed" (c.

meanings.definitionCn: 个性； 性格； 名人

morphology.mnemonic: person + 与…有关的/形容词 + ity

etymology.story: late 14c., "quality or fact of being a person," from Medieval Latin personalitatem (nominative personalitas), from Late Latin personalis (see personal).

meanings.definitionCn: 专门用语， 术语； 术语学

morphology.mnemonic: 终止/界限 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: 1770, from German Terminologie, a hybrid coined by Christian Gottfried Schütz (1747-1832), professor of poetry and rhetoric at Jena, from Medieval Latin terminus "word, expression" (see terminus) + Greek -logia "a dealing with, a speaking of" (see -logy).
"on the watch," 1590s, from French alerte "vigilant" (17c.), from phrase à l'erte "on the watch," from Italian all'erta "to the height," from erta "lookout, high tower," noun use of fem.

meanings.definitionCn: 估价； 评级， 评价； 把…列为

etymology.story: "estimated value or worth," early 15c., from Old French rate "price, value" and directly from Medieval Latin rata (pars) "fixed (amount)," from Latin rata "fixed, settled," fem.

meanings.definitionCn: 同意，许可；惩罚，实施制裁
同意， 许可； 约束； 制裁

morphology.mnemonic: 神圣 + 状态/行为/过程

1630s, "owner, by royal grant, of an American colony," probably from proprietary (n.) in sense "property owner" (late 15c., see proprietary).
mid-14c., from Anglo-French contrarie, from Latin contrarius "opposite, opposed," from contra "against" (see contra).

etymology.story: 1939, a hybrid coined from English pest + Latinate -cide..

etymology.story: late 14c., "thick, sweet liquid," from Old French sirop "sugared drink" (13c.), and perhaps from Italian siroppo, both from Arabic sharab "beverage, wine," literally "something drunk," from verb shariba "he drank" (compare sherbet).
"go against," late 14c., from Old French countre "facing opposite" (see counter-).

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 保持/服务

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French conserver (9c.), from Latin conservare "to keep, preserve, keep intact, guard," from com-, intensive prefix (see com-), + servare "keep watch, maintain" (see observe).
"of the Goths," the ancient Germanic people, "pertaining to the Goths or their language," 1610s, from Late Latin Gothicus, from Gothi, Greek Gothoi (see Goth).

1550s, "rhymed verse, metrical movement," from Latin rhythmus "movement in time," from Greek rhythmos "measured flow or movement, rhythm; proportion, symmetry; arrangement, order; form, shape, wise, manner; soul, disposition," related to rhein "to flow," from PIE root *sreu- "to flow" (see rheum).

meanings.definitionCn: 疹， 皮疹； 一连串令人不快的事物

meanings.definitionCn: 陶器； 陶器厂， 制陶作坊； 制陶工艺， 陶器制造术

etymology.story: 1560s, from Middle French cylindre (14c.), from Latin cylindrus "roller, cylinder," from Greek kylindros "a cylinder, roller, roll," from kylindein "to roll," which is of unknown origin..

meanings.definitionCn: 革除， 罢免， 废黜

etymology.story: mid-15c., "fortified place, stronghold," from Old French fort "fort, fortress; strong man," noun use of adjective meaning "strong, stout, sturdy; hard, severe, difficult; hard to understand; dreadful, terrible; fortified" (10c.), from Latin fortis "strong, mighty; firm, steadfast; brave, spirited," from Old Latin forctus, possibly from PIE root *bhergh- (2) "high, elevated," with derivatives referring to hills and hill-forts (see barrow (n.2)).

meanings.definitionCn: 使…续期； 重新开始； 重申； 修复

meanings.definitionCn: 憔悴； 凋谢， 枯萎； 受煎熬

meanings.definitionCn: 立法的， 有立法权的； 根据法规执行的

early 15c., from Latin intricatus "entangled," past participle of intricare "to entangle, perplex, embarrass," from in- "in" (see in- (2)) + tricae (plural) "perplexities, hindrances, toys, tricks," a word of uncertain origin (compare extricate).

meanings.definitionCn: 思量， 考虑， 思忖； 注视， 凝视； 打算

1835 (as an adjective 1833), from Modern Latin Rodentia, the order name, from Latin rodentem (nominative rodens), present participle of rodere "to gnaw, eat away," from PIE root *red- "to scrape, scratch, gnaw" (source also of Sanskrit radati "scrapes, gnaws," radanah "tooth;" Latin radere "to scrape;" Welsh rhathu "scrape, polish").

morphology.mnemonic: 在…之下 + 站立/设置 + ance

1822, "advocate of extension of the political franchise in Britain," without regard to gender, or, in the U.S., of voting rights for free blacks; from suffrage + -ist.

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French melodios "melodious; delightful" (French mélodieux), from Medieval Latin melodiosus, from Latin melodia (see melody).

late 14c., from Old French ableté "expert at handling (something)," from Latin habilitatem (nominative habilitas) "aptitude," noun of quality from habilis "easy to manage, handy" (see able).

1640s, from Latin continuum "a continuous thing," neuter of continuus (see continue).

平局， 和局； 抽签

meanings.definitionCn: 口头的， 口述的； 口腔的

etymology.story: 1620s, from Late Latin oralis, from Latin os (genitive oris) "mouth, opening, face, entrance," from PIE *os- "mouth" (source also of Sanskrit asan "mouth," asyam "mouth, opening," Avestan ah-, Hittite aish, Middle Irish a "mouth," Old Norse oss "mouth of a river," Old English or "beginning, origin, front").

1640s, "to be equivalent;" 1650s, "to counterbalance, make up for," from Latin compensatus, past participle of compensare "to weigh one thing (against another)," thus, "to counterbalance," from com- "with" (see com-) + pensare, frequentative of pendere "to weigh" (see pendant).

meanings.definitionCn: 成就； 完成； 才艺， 技艺

1580s, "to support or represent in a duel, fight, etc.," from Middle French seconder, from Latin secundare "to assist, make favorable," from secundus "assisting, favorable, following, second" (see second (adj.)).

etymology.story: Old English fæstnian "make fast, make firm, fix, secure," also "ratify, betroth, confirm," from Proto-Germanic *fastinon "to make firm or fast" (source also of Old Frisian festnia "to make firm, bind fast," Old Saxon fastnon, Old High German fastnion, German festnen, Old Norse fastna "to pledge, betroth"), from PIE *fast "solid, firm" (see fast (adj.)).

1660s, from French toxique and directly from Late Latin toxicus "poisoned," from Latin toxicum "poison," from Greek toxikon (pharmakon) "(poison) for use on arrows," from toxikon, neuter of toxikos "pertaining to arrows or archery," and thus to a bow, from toxon "bow," probably from a Scythian word that also was borrowed into Latin as taxus "yew." Watkins suggests a possible source in Iranian taxša- "bow," from PIE *tekw- "to run, flee." As a noun from 1890..

竞赛； 辩论； 争夺

Attested earlier in English as a verb (1510s), though this now is obsolete..

meanings.definitionCn: 握紧， 抓牢； 咬紧

1590s, from re- "back, again" + kindle (v.).

1610s, "anything spread out;" 1640s, "act of expanding," from French expansion, from Late Latin expansionem (nominative expansio) "a spreading out," noun of action from past participle stem of Latin expandere "to spread out" (see expand)..

meanings.definitionCn: 扭伤； 痛苦， 难受； 扳手

Squander-bug, a British symbol of reckless extravagance and waste during war-time shortages, represented as a devilish insect, was introduced 1943.

meanings.definitionCn: 海豹； 封铅， 封条； 印， 图章
库房； 贮藏； 存储

root.meaning: to keep in store for future use

morphology.mnemonic: to keep in store for future use + 行为/结果/集合

etymology.story: 1610s, "space for storing," from store (v.) + -age.

1914, from French troposphère, literally "sphere of change," coined by French meteorologist Philippe Teisserenc de Bort (1855-1913) from Greek tropos "a turn, change" (see trope) + sphaira "sphere" (see sphere).
1782, back-formation from swindler "cheater."

meanings.definitionCn: 幻觉， 错觉； 幻想中的事物

morphology.mnemonic: 向前/支持 + 引导 + 有…倾向的 + ity

Italian cugino, Danish kusine, Polish kuzyn also are from French.
