etymology.story: Old English aflote, on flot, from a- "on" (see a- (1)) + flot "body of water" (see float (n.))..

1590s, "slender riding whip," probably from a Flemish or Low German word akin to Middle Dutch swijch "bough, twig," or swutsche, variant of Low German zwukse "long thin stick, switch," from Germanic *swih- (source also of Old High German zwec "wooden peg," German Zweck "aim, design," originally "peg as a target," Zwick "wooden peg"), perhaps connected with PIE root *swei- (2) "to swing, bend, to turn."

The meaning "device for changing the direction of something or making or breaking a connection" is first recorded 1797.

meanings.definitionCn: 攻击的；冒犯的；无礼的；讨厌的

morphology.mnemonic: action of attacking + 有…倾向的

meanings.definitionCn: 质地；纹理；结构；本质，实质
发射（导弹、火箭等）；发起，发动；使…下水
发射；发行，投放市场；下水；汽艇
制止， 镇压； 缓解， 减轻

etymology.story: Old English cwellan "to kill, murder, execute," from Proto-Germanic *kwaljanan (source also of Old English cwelan "to die," cwalu "violent death;" Old Saxon quellian "to torture, kill;" Old Norse kvelja "to torment;" Middle Dutch quelen "to vex, tease, torment;" Old High German quellan "to suffer pain," German quälen "to torment, torture"), from PIE root *gwele- (1) "to throw, reach," with extended sense of "to pierce" (source also of Armenian kelem "I torture;" Old Church Slavonic zali "pain;" Lithuanian galas "end," gela "agony," gelti "to sting;" see ballistics).

meanings.definitionCn: 少说，少报；保守地说；有意轻描淡写

etymology.story: 1781, from under + state (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 确认；确定；证实；批准；使巩固

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 坚固/稳定

etymology.story: mid-13c., confirmyn "to ratify," from Old French confermer (13c., Modern French confirmer) "strengthen, establish, consolidate; affirm by proof or evidence; anoint (a king)," from Latin confirmare "make firm, strengthen, establish," from com-, intensive prefix (see com-), + firmare "to strengthen," from firmus "strong, steadfast" (see firm (adj.)).
mid-15c., from upper (adj.) + -most..

meanings.definitionCn: 迅速而有效的， 迅速完成的

morphology.mnemonic: 出/向外/前任 + 脚/儿童 + ite + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: late 15c., expedycius "useful, fitting," from Latin expeditus "disengaged, ready, convenient, prompt; unfettered, unencumbered," past participle of expedire (see expedite).

meanings.definitionCn: 飞驰；急速进行；急急忙忙地说

morphology.mnemonic: 新 + ic + 与…有关的/形容词
死亡 + 与…有关的/形容词 + ity

etymology.story: mid-14c., "condition of being mortal," from Old French mortalite "massacre, slaughter; fatal illness; poverty; destruction" (12c.), from Latin mortalitem (nominative mortalitas) "state of being mortal; subjection to death," from mortalis (see mortal (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 广阔的；巨大的；大量的；巨额的

etymology.story: 1570s, "being of great extent or size," from Middle French vaste, from Latin vastus "immense, extensive, huge," also "desolate, unoccupied, empty." The two meanings probably originally attached to two separate words, one with a long -a- one with a short -a-, that merged in early Latin (see waste (v.)).
early 15c., from Latin dissentire "differ in sentiments, disagree, be at odds, contradict, quarrel," from dis- "differently" (see dis-) + sentire "to feel, think" (see sense (n.)).

early 15c., "creed, summary, religious belief," from Late Latin symbolum "creed, token, mark," from Greek symbolon "token, watchword, sign by which one infers; ticket, a permit, license" (the word was applied c.250 by Cyprian of Carthage to the Apostles' Creed, on the notion of the "mark" that distinguishes Christians from pagans), literally "that which is thrown or cast together," from assimilated form of syn- "together" (see syn-) + bole "a throwing, a casting, the stroke of a missile, bolt, beam," from bol-, nominative stem of ballein "to throw" (see ballistics).
1660s (trans.), from Latin ignitus, past participle of ignire "set on fire, make red hot," from ignis "fire" (see igneous).

early 14c., from Old French declarer "explain, elucidate," or directly from Latin declarare "make clear, reveal, disclose, announce," from de- intensive prefix (see de-) + clarare "clarify," from clarus "clear" (see clear (adj.)).
1540s, from French calamiteux (16c.), from Latin calamitosus "causing loss, destructive," from calamitas (see calamity).

meanings.definitionCn: 可用的， 可得到的；可以见到的， 随时可来的

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + 强壮/价值 + able

etymology.story: mid-15c., "beneficial," also "valid, effective, capable of producing the desired effect," from avail + -able.

meanings.definitionCn: 不同的， 不相等的； 不平等的， 不相称的； 不胜任的

morphology.mnemonic: 不/非/否定 + equi + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1530s, "unjust, unfair," from un- (1) "not" + equal (adj.).

meanings.definitionCn: 限制；使不外出，禁闭n.[pl.]界限，范围

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + to punish by pecuniary penalty

etymology.story: 1520s, "to border on," from Middle French confiner, from confins (n.); see confine (n.).

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + 呼叫/声音 + 使…/具有…的

meanings.definitionCn: 遗腹的；父亡后出生的；死后的；身后的

morphology.mnemonic: 在…之后 + hum + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: mid-15c., "born after the death of the originator" (author or father), from Late Latin posthumus, from Latin postumus "last, last-born," superlative of posterus "coming after, subsequent" (see posterior).

meanings.definitionCn: 水平的；地平线的；同一阶层的
水平线，水平面；水平位置

etymology.story: 1550s, "relating to or near the horizon," from French horizontal, from Latin horizontem (see horizon).
"hindrance, disadvantage,"1720, from draw (v.) + back (adv.).

meanings.definitionCn: 导弹的；可投掷的；用以发射导弹的

etymology.story: late 12c., "sufferings of Christ on the Cross," from Old French passion "Christ's passion, physical suffering" (10c.), from Late Latin passionem (nominative passio) "suffering, enduring," from past participle stem of Latin pati "to suffer, endure," possibly from PIE root *pe(i)- "to hurt" (see fiend).

meanings.definitionCn: 极端的；极度的；偏激的；尽头的
极端；末端；最大程度；极端的事物
轶话的， 轶闻趣事的

morphology.mnemonic: 统治 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1794, from anecdote + -al (1).

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 顺序/等级 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1660s, "to place in the same rank," from Latin coordinare (see coordination).

meanings.definitionCn: 运动员，体育家；身强力壮的人

etymology.story: early 15c., from Latin athleta "a wrestler, athlete, combatant in public games," from Greek athletes "prizefighter, contestant in the games," agent noun from athlein "to contest for a prize," related to athlos "a contest" and athlon "a prize," which is of unknown origin.

meanings.definitionCn: 抢夺， 掠夺； 蹂躏， 破坏
疑问，质问；疑问号 ；[计] 查询

etymology.story: early 13c., from Old French degré (12c.) "a step (of a stair), pace, degree (of relationship), academic degree; rank, status, position," said to be from Vulgar Latin *degradus "a step," from Late Latin degredare, from Latin de- "down" (see de-) + gradus "step" (see grade (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 阴沉的, 凄凉的n沼泽
用言语表达； 说， 唱， 发声

morphology.mnemonic: 呼叫/声音 + 与…有关的/形容词 + ize

etymology.story: 1660s, from vocal + -ize.

morphology.mnemonic: 星 + 具有…特征/名词化

meanings.definitionCn: 使成管状；把…装管；用管输送

etymology.story: 1610s, from Middle French tube (15c.), from Latin tubus "tube, pipe," of unknown origin.
"very small wave," 1798, from earlier meaning "stretch of shallow, rippling water" (1755), from ripple (v.).

morphology.mnemonic: 船 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: early 15c., from Old French naval (14c.) and directly from Latin navalis "pertaining to a ship or ships," from navis "ship," from PIE *nau- (2) "boat" (source also of Sanskrit nauh, accusative navam "ship, boat;" Armenian nav "ship;" Greek naus "ship," nautes "sailor;" Old Irish nau "ship;" Welsh noe "a flat vessel;" Old Norse nor "ship").

meanings.definitionCn: 近视的； 缺乏远见的

etymology.story: "short-sighted," 1800, from myopia + -ic.
1630s, from assimilated form of in- (1) "not, opposite of" + legible.

morphology.mnemonic: 向下/移除/取消 + animal of vast size + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1550s, "to point out," from Latin demonstratus, past participle of demonstrare "to indicate, describe" (see demonstration).

morphology.mnemonic: 向下/移除/取消 + 共同/联合 + 放置

etymology.story: 1750s, "to separate into components," from de- "opposite of" + compose.

meanings.definitionCn: 纪念碑,纪念馆,遗迹,不朽的业绩

etymology.story: late 13c., "a sepulchre," from Old French monument "grave, tomb, monument," and directly from Latin monumentum "a monument, memorial structure, statue; votive offering; tomb; memorial record," literally "something that reminds," from monere "to remind, warn" (see monitor (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 详细目录,存货,财产清册,总量
鲜艳的;生动的,栩栩如生的

etymology.story: 1630s, from French vivide and perhaps also directly from Latin vividus "spirited, animated, lively, full of life," from vivus "alive," from PIE *gweie- (1) "to live" (see bio-).
as a music form featuring flatted thirds and sevenths, possibly c.
1570s, "to purify," from Latin defaecatus, past participle of defaecare "cleanse from dregs, purify," from the phrase de faece "from dregs" (plural faeces; see feces).
early 14c., "consent, approval," from Old French assent, a back-formation from assentir (see assent (v.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 新闻报道； 覆盖范围

root.meaning: recording of a song already recorded by another

morphology.mnemonic: recording of a song already recorded by another + 行为/结果/集合

etymology.story: mid-15c., "charge for a booth at a fair," from cover + -age.

meanings.definitionCn: (尤指食物)易坏的,易腐烂的;易坏之物"

etymology.story: late 15c., perysabyl, from Middle French périssable, and later (in modern form), 1610s, directly from perish + -able.
early 15c., "having steps or ridges," from Medieval Latin gradualis, from Latin gradus "step" (see grade (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 数字；人物；图形；价格；（人的）体形；画像

etymology.story: late 14c., "to represent" (in painting or sculpture), "make a likeness," also "to have a certain shape or appearance," from Old French figurer, from Latin figurare (see figure (n.)).
mid-14c., "contaminate, impair the purity of," from Latin corruptus, past participle of corrumpere (see corrupt (adj.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + memory记忆 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1590s, from Latin commemoratus, past participle of commemorare "bring to remembrance" (see commemoration).

meanings.definitionCn: 传输；传播；发射；传达；遗传

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + sume + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., "wasting of the body by disease; wasting disease" (replacing Old English yfeladl "the evil disease"), from Old French consumpcion, from Latin consumptionem (nominative consumptio) "a using up, wasting," noun of state from past participle stem of consumere (see consume).

1550s, "an index, list, catalogue," from Late Latin repertorium "inventory, list," from Latin repertus, past participle of reperire "to find, get, invent," from re-, intensive prefix (see re-), + parire, archaic form of paerere "produce, bring forth," from PIE root *pere- (1) "to bring forth" (see pare).

Old English hæsl, hæsel, from Proto-Germanic *hasalaz (source also of Old Norse hasl, Middle Dutch hasel, German hasel), from PIE *koselo- "hazel" (source also of Latin corulus, Old Irish coll "hazel").
"matter used in vaccination," 1846, from French vaccin, noun use of adjective, from Latin vaccina, fem.

meanings.definitionCn: 乐趣；滋味，风味; 兴趣

etymology.story: 1670s, from French zeste "piece of orange or lemon peel used as a flavoring," of unknown origin.

Old English drugað, drugoð "drought, dryness, desert," from Proto-Germanic *drugothaz, from Germanic root *dreug- "dry" (cf high/height) with *-itho, Germanic suffix for forming abstract nouns (see -th (2)).

root.meaning: dot marking end of a sentence

morphology.mnemonic: dot marking end of a sentence + ic + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1839, American English, poker term, perhaps from Dutch bluffen "to brag, boast," or verbluffen "to baffle, mislead." An identical word meant "blindfold, hoodwink" in 1670s, but the sense evolution and connection are unclear; OED calls it "one of the numerous cant terms ...

early 15c., "to take notice of," from Middle French advertiss-, present participle stem of a(d)vertir "to warn" (12c.), from Latin advertere "turn toward," from ad- "toward" (see ad-) + vertere "to turn" (see versus).

also yoghurt, 1620s, a mispronunciation of Turkish yogurt, in which the -g- is a "soft" sound, in many dialects closer to an English "w." The root yog means roughly "to condense" and is related to yogun "intense," yogush "liquify" (of water vapor), yogur "knead.".
mid-15c., from Middle French comparatif, from Latin comparativus "pertaining to comparison," from comparat-, past participle stem of comparare (see comparison).

meanings.definitionCn: 迟疑的；踌躇的；犹豫不定的

etymology.story: 1640s, probably a back-formation from hesitancy, or else from Latin haesitantem.
1920, in the electronics sense, "the return of a fraction of an output signal to the input of an earlier stage," from verbal phrase, from feed (v.) + back (adv.).
"traditional beliefs and customs of the common people," 1846, coined by antiquarian William J.
"that which entitles to credit," 1756, probably a back-formation from credentials.
1570s, "examine again," from re- + view (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 确立，牢固；用壕沟围住；挖掘

etymology.story: also intrench, 1550s, implied in intrenched, from en- (1) "make, put in" + trench (n.).

Old English timber "building, structure," in late Old English "building material, trees suitable for building," and "trees or woods in general," from Proto-Germanic *timran (source also of Old Saxon timbar "a building, room," Old Frisian timber "wood, building," Old High German zimbar "timber, wooden dwelling, room," Old Norse timbr "timber," German Zimmer "room"), from PIE *deme- "to build," possibly from root *dem- "house, household" (source of Greek domos, Latin domus; see domestic (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 随便走，漫游；徜徉；漫谈某事

morphology.mnemonic: 相似/相同 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1620s, "feign, pretend, assume falsely" (implied in simulated), back-formation from simulation or else from Latin simulatus, past participle of simulare "to make like, imitate, copy." Meaning "to use a model to imitate certain conditions for purposes of study or training" is from 1947.

"principle, opinion, or dogma maintained as true by a person, sect, school, etc.," properly "a thing held (to be true)," early 15c., from Latin tenet "he holds," third person singular present indicative of tenere "to hold, grasp, keep, have possession, maintain," also "reach, gain, acquire, obtain; hold back, repress, restrain;" figuratively "hold in mind, take in, understand."

The Latin word is from PIE root *ten- "to stretch" (source also of Sanskrit tantram "loom," tanoti "stretches, lasts;" Persian tar "string;" Lithuanian tankus "compact," i.e.

mid-14c., "specially prepared or arranged display," from Old French spectacle "sight, spectacle, Roman games" (13c.), from Latin spectaculum "a public show, spectacle, place from which shows are seen," from spectare "to view, watch, behold," frequentative form of specere "to look at," from PIE *spek- "to observe" (see scope (n.1))..
1590s, "power to accomplish something," from Latin efficientia "efficient power; efficiency; influence" (from efficientem; see efficient) + -cy.
late 13c., probably from Old French laitues, plural of laitue "lettuce," from Latin lactuca "lettuce," from lac (genitive lactis) "milk" (see lacto-); so called for the milky juice of the plant..

meanings.definitionCn: 完整无缺的,未经触动的,未受损伤的

etymology.story: mid-15c., from Latin intactus "untouched, uninjured; undefiled, chaste; unsubdued," from in- "not" (see in- (1)) + tactus, past participle of tangere "to touch" (see tangent (adj.))..
1580s, from Italian motto "a saying, legend attached to a heraldic design," from Late Latin muttum "grunt, word," from Latin muttire "to mutter, mumble, murmur" (see mutter)..

1620s, "young, youthful," from Latin iuvenilis "of or belonging to youth, youthful," from iuvenis "young person, one in the flower of his or her age" (in Roman use, the period just beyond adolescence, from age 21 or 25 to 40), noun use of an adjective meaning "young" (source also of French jeune; see young (adj.)).
1750, from Latin pervas-, past participle stem of pervadere (see pervade) + -ive..

meanings.definitionCn: 蜂房，蜂箱；蜂房内的蜂群；闹市，忙碌之地

morphology.mnemonic: 劳动/工作 + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: late 14c., "hard-working, industrious," from Old French laborios "arduous, wearisome; hard-working" (12c., Modern French laborieux), from Latin laboriosus "toilsome, wearisome, troublesome," also "inclined to labor, industrious," from labor "toil, exertion" (see labor (n.)).
1670s, verbal noun from race (v.)..

meanings.definitionCn: 有同情心的；赞同的；n.交感神经

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/一起 + 感情/疾病 + ic

etymology.story: 1640s, "pertaining to sympathy," from Modern Latin sympatheticus, from late Greek sympathetikos "having sympathy," from sympathein, from sympathes "having a fellow feeling, affected by like feelings" (see sympathy).
"to load (a ship) with goods or merchandise for shipment," mid-15c.

meanings.definitionCn: （在内）变动；平行，列为一行；延伸；漫游；射程达到

etymology.story: also top-soil, 1789, from top (adj.) + soil (n.)..

late 14c., from Latin Aurora, the Roman goddess of dawn, from PIE *ausus- "dawn," also the name of the Indo-European goddess of the dawn, from root *aus- (1) "to shine," especially of the dawn (source also of Greek eos "dawn," auein "to dry, kindle;" Sanskrit usah, Lithuanian ausra "dawn;" Latin auster "south wind," usum "to burn;" Old English east "east")..
1875, from glamour + -ous, with typical dropping of the -u- in derivatives (see -or).
late 14c., from Old French desprover "refute, contradict," from des- (see dis-) + prover (see prove).

late 14c., from Old French deriver "to flow, pour out; derive, originate," from Latin derivare "to lead or draw off (a stream of water) from its source" (in Late Latin also "to derive"), from phrase de rivo (de "from" + rivus "stream;" see rivulet).

mid-15c., "pleasure," from Medieval Latin complacentia "satisfaction, pleasure," from Latin complacentem (nominative complacens), present participle of complacere "to be very pleasing," from com-, intensive prefix (see com-), + placere "to please" (see please).

meanings.definitionCn: 维护，坚持；断言；主张；声称
拍打，拍打声；神经紧张；[航] 襟翼
拍动；神经紧张；鼓翼而飞；（帽边等）垂下

etymology.story: early 14c., "dash about, shake, beat (the wings);" later "strike, hit" (mid-14c.); probably ultimately imitative.

meanings.definitionCn: 定期的；有规律的；合格的；整齐的；普通的
常客；正式队员；中坚分子
冰期的； 冰川的； 寒冷的， 冰冷的； 冷若冰霜的

etymology.story: 1650s, "cold, icy," from French glacial or directly from Latin glacialis "icy, frozen, full of ice," from glacies "ice," probably from a suffixed form of PIE root *gel- (2) "cold, to freeze" (source also of Latin gelu "frost;" see cold (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 美丽如画的； 生动的

morphology.mnemonic: paint画，描绘 + 行为/过程/结果 + esque

etymology.story: 1703, on pattern of French pittoresque, a loan-word from Italian pittoresco, literally "pictorial" (1660s), from pittore "painter," from Latin pictorem (nominative pictor); see painter (n.1).

meanings.definitionCn: 有学识的； 见多识广的， 有见识的

etymology.story: 1540s, "current in information," past-participle adjective from inform (v.).

morphology.mnemonic: 做/驱动 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1610s, "unprovoked attack," from French aggression (16c.), from Latin aggressionem (nominative aggressio) "a going to, an attack," noun of action from past participle stem of aggredi "to approach; attack," from ad- "to" (see ad-) + gradi (past participle gressus) "to step," from gradus "a step" (see grade (n.)).
1610s, from re- "again" + furbish, on model of French refourbir.

meanings.definitionCn: 高低不平的, 崎岖的
碎片,小部分,一点儿;分数

morphology.mnemonic: 打破 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., originally in the mathematical sense, from Anglo-French fraccioun (Old French fraccion, "a breaking," 12c., Modern French fraction) and directly from Late Latin fractionem (nominative fractio) "a breaking," especially into pieces, in Medieval Latin "a fragment, portion," noun of action from past participle stem of Latin frangere "to break (something) in pieces, shatter, fracture," from Proto-Italic *frang-, from a nasalized variant of PIE root *bhreg- "to break" (source also of Sanskrit (giri)-bhraj "breaking-forth (out of the mountains);" Gothic brikan, Old English brecan "to break;" Lithuanian brasketi "crash, crack;" Old Irish braigim "break wind").

meanings.definitionCn: 象征的；表意的；作为对某事的保证的
滤波器；[化工] 过滤器；筛选；滤光器

etymology.story: 1570s (transitive), from French filtrer or from Medieval Latin filtrare, from filtrum "felt" (see filter (n.)).

1550s, "wait, defer action," from Latin expectare/exspectare "await, look out for; desire, hope, long for, anticipate; look for with anticipation," from ex- "thoroughly" (see ex-) + spectare "to look," frequentative of specere "to look at" (see scope (n.1)).

1570s, "a (social) moving, stirring, agitation," from Middle French émotion (16c.), from Old French emouvoir "stir up" (12c.), from Latin emovere "move out, remove, agitate," from assimilated form of ex- "out" (see ex-) + movere "to move" (see move (v.)).
Old English soðian "show to be true," from soð "true" (see sooth).

early 15c., "to fix the amount (of a tax, fine, etc.)," from Anglo-French assesser, from Medieval Latin assessare "fix a tax upon," originally frequentative of Latin assessus "a sitting by," past participle of assidere "to sit beside" (and thus to assist in the office of a judge), from ad- "to" (see ad-) + sedere "to sit" (see sedentary).
1750, from French étiquette "prescribed behavior," from Old French estiquette "label, ticket" (see ticket (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 在…之前/预先 + 写 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., in law, "the right to something through long use," from Old French prescription (13c.) and directly from Latin praescriptionem (nominative praescriptio) "a writing before, order, direction," noun of action from past participle stem of praescribere "write before, prefix in writing; ordain, determine in advance," from prae "before" (see pre-) + scribere "to write" (see script (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 看/观察 + 使…/具有…的 + …者/…器

etymology.story: 1580s, from Latin spectator "viewer, watcher," from past participle stem of spectare "to view, watch" (see spectacle).

meanings.definitionCn: 投降；提交（物）；服从；（向法官提出的）意见；谦恭

etymology.story: late 14c., "act of referring to a third party for judgment or decision," from Old French submission or directly from Latin submissionem (nominative submissio) "a lowering, letting down; sinking," noun of action from past participle stem of submittere "to let down, put down, lower, reduce, yield" (see submit).

meanings.definitionCn: 完全的；彻底的；无条件的
充足的，足够的；适当的，胜任的

etymology.story: 1610s, from Latin adaequatus "equalized," past participle of adaequare "to make equal to," from ad- "to" (see ad-) + aequare "make level," from aequus (see equal (adj.)).
1771, "the total of the animal life of a certain region or time, from Late Latin Fauna, a rustic Roman fertility goddess who was wife, sister, or daughter (or some combination) of Faunus (see faun).

morphology.mnemonic: 一/单一 + 形状/形态 + ity

etymology.story: early 15c., from Old French uniformite (14c.) or directly from Late Latin uniformitatem (nominative uniformitas) "uniformity," from Latin uniformis (see uniform (adj.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 喧闹；活跃；裙撑；热闹的活动

etymology.story: "be active," 1570s (bustling "noisy or excited activity" is from early 15c.), frequentative of Middle English bresten "to rush, break," from Old English bersten (see burst (v.)), influenced by Old Norse buask "to make oneself ready" (see busk (v.)), or it might be from busk (v.) via a frequentative form buskle.

meanings.definitionCn: 极大的，巨大的；惊人的；极好的

etymology.story: 1630s, "awful, dreadful, terrible," from Latin tremendus "fearful, to be dreaded, terrible," literally "to be trembled at," gerundive form of tremere "to tremble" (see tremble (v.)).

early 15c., from Middle French condenser (14c.) or directly from Latin condensare "to make dense," from com-, intensive prefix (see com-), + densare "make thick," from densus "dense, thick, crowded," a word used of crowds, darkness, clouds, etc.

morphology.mnemonic: any kind of corporeal matter + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1650s, "to make real, to give substance to," from Modern Latin substantiatus, past participle of substantiare, from Latin substantia "being, essence, material" (see substance).

1670s, from French hygiène, ultimately from Greek hygieine techne "the healthful art," from hygies "healthy, sound, hearty," literally "living well" (personified as the goddess Hygieia), from PIE *eyu-gwie-es- "having a vigorous life," from root *aiw-, *ayu- "vital force, life, long life, eternity; in the prime of life, young" (source of Latin aevus, English ever; see eon).

1857, title given by foreigners to the shogun of Japan (said to have been used by his supporters when addressing foreigners, as an attempt to convey that the shogun was more important than the emperor), from Japanese taikun "great lord or prince," from Chinese tai "great" + kiun "lord." Transferred meaning "important person" is attested from 1861, in reference to U.S.
1540s, from Latin conspicuus "visible, open to view, striking," from conspicere "to look at, observe, see, notice," from com-, intensive prefix (see com-), + specere (see scope (n.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: 司法，法律制裁；正义；法官，审判员

etymology.story: mid-12c., "the exercise of authority in vindication of right by assigning reward or punishment;" also "quality of being fair and just; moral soundness and conformity to truth," from Old French justice "justice, legal rights, jurisdiction" (11c.), from Latin iustitia "righteousness, equity," from iustus "upright, just" (see just (adj.)).

late Old English (replacing Old English fers, an early West Germanic borrowing directly from Latin), "line or section of a psalm or canticle," later "line of poetry" (late 14c.), from Anglo-French and Old French vers "line of verse; rhyme, song," from Latin versus "a line, row, line of verse, line of writing," from PIE root *wer- (3) "to turn, bend" (see versus).

late 14c., "impetuous, unrestrained," from Old French furios, furieus "furious, enraged, livid" (14c., Modern French furieux), from Latin furiosus "full of rage, mad," from furia "rage, passion, fury" (see fury).

morphology.mnemonic: 不/进入 + watershed, separation between river valleys + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: early 13c., from Anglo-French samoun, Old French salmun (Modern French saumon), from Latin salmonem (nominative salmo) "a salmon," probably originally "leaper," from salire "to leap" (see salient (adj.)), though some dismiss this as folk etymology.

meanings.definitionCn: 花束； 香味， 芬芳

etymology.story: 1716, introduced to English by Lady Mary Montague from French bouquet, originally "little wood," from Picard form of Old French bochet (14c.), diminutive of bosco, from Medieval Latin boscus "grove" (see bush (n.))..

morphology.mnemonic: animal faculty of intuitive perception + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1841, from instinct (Latin instinctus) + -al (1).

early 14c., constreyen, from stem of Old French constreindre (Modern French contraindre) "restrain, control," from Latin constringere "to bind together, tie tightly, fetter, shackle, chain," from com- "together" (see com-) + stringere "to draw tight" (see strain (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 皇后, 女皇帝, 皇太后, 极有权力的女人

etymology.story: mid-12c., emperice, from Old French emperesse, fem.

meanings.definitionCn: 提供消息或情报的人， 线人； 提供资料的人

etymology.story: 1690s, "someone who supplies information," from Latin informantem (nominative informans), present participle of informare "train, instruct, educate" (see inform).

meanings.definitionCn: 临时的，暂时的；中间的；间歇的
过渡时期，中间时期；暂定

etymology.story: "time intervening," 1560s, from Latin interim (adv.) "in the meantime, meanwhile," originally "in the midst of that," from inter- "between" (see inter-) + im, ancient adverb from stem of pronoun is "this, that." As an adjective from c.

morphology.mnemonic: 使成为/放入 + bring about by unusual effort

etymology.story: mid-14c., "to drive by physical force; to try, attempt, strive; to fortify, strengthen a place;" late 14c.

meanings.definitionCn: 注定的； 以…为目的地的
易碎的，脆的，易损坏的；虚弱的，脆弱的

etymology.story: 1510s, "liable to sin, morally weak;" c.

meanings.definitionCn: 需细心的，辛苦的，煞费苦心的；勤勉的；刻苦的
辛苦； 勤勉； 刻苦

etymology.story: 1550s (n.), 1690s (adj.), paynes taking, from plural of pain (n.) + present participle of take (v.).

the science of social phenomena, 1842, from French sociologie, a hybrid coined 1830 by French philosopher Isidore Auguste Comte (1798-1857), from Latin socius "associate" (see social (adj.)) + Greek-derived suffix -logie (see -logy)..

meanings.definitionCn: 发胖的， 肥胖的， 强壮的； 结实的， 牢固的； 顽强的， 坚毅的

etymology.story: 1610s, "pertaining to giants," from Latin gigant- stem of gigas "giant" (see giant) + -ic.

meanings.definitionCn: 明显的；独特的；清楚的；有区别的

etymology.story: late 14c., originally past participle of distincten (c.

meanings.definitionCn: 用树篱笆围住；避免作正面答复
对冲，套期保值；树篱；障碍

etymology.story: 1620s, from dragon + fly (n.)..

meanings.definitionCn: 意识到的；知道的；有…方面知识的；懂世故的

etymology.story: late Old English gewær, from Proto-Germanic *ga-waraz (source also of Old Saxon giwar, Middle Dutch gheware, Old High German giwar, German gewahr), from *ga-, intensive prefix, + waraz "wary, cautious" (see wary)..

meanings.definitionCn: 符合条件的;(尤指婚姻等)合适(意)的

etymology.story: early 15c., "fit or proper to be chosen," from Old French eligible "fit to be chosen" (14c.), from Late Latin eligibilis "that may be chosen," from Latin eligere "choose" (see election).
"to clear the ground of weeds," late Old English weodian "to weed," from the source of weed (n.).

1744, from French glacier (16c.), from Savoy dialect glacière "moving mass of ice," from Old French glace "ice," from Vulgar Latin *glacia (source also of Old Provençal glassa, Italian ghiaccia), from Latin glacies "ice" (see glacial).
"final demand," 1731, from Modern Latin, from Medieval Latin ultimatum "a final statement," noun use of Latin adjective ultimatum "last possible, final," neuter of ultimatus (see ultimate).
also rain-fall, "amount of precipitation that falls as rain," 1839, from rain (n.) + fall (n.)..

meanings.definitionCn: 种族主义， 种族歧视

morphology.mnemonic: run swiftly + 主义/学说/运动

meanings.definitionCn: 方向；定向；适应；情况介绍；向东方

morphology.mnemonic: determine bearings + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1839, originally "arrangement of a building, etc., to face east or any other specified direction," noun of action from orient (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 空闲的， 闲置的；无用的， 无效的；懒散的

etymology.story: "to pledge" (obsolete except in archaic plight one's troth), from Old English pligtan, plihtan "to endanger, imperil, compromise," verb form of pliht (n.) "danger, risk," from PIE root *dlegh- "to engage oneself," forming words in Celtic, Germanic, Slavic, and possibly Latin (see play (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 使比赛；使相配；敌得过，比得上；相配；与…竞争
比赛，竞赛；匹配；对手；火柴
故障；[地质] 断层；错误；缺点；毛病；（网球等）发球失误
相互作用， 相互影响

etymology.story: 1838, from inter- "between" + play (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 致敬；招呼；向欢呼；猛发；使像下雹样落下（过去式hailed，过去分词hailed，现在分词hailing，第三人称单数hails）

etymology.story: "frozen rain, pellets of ice falling in showers," Old English hægl, hagol (Mercian hegel) "hail, hailstorm," also the name of the rune for H, from Proto-Germanic *haglaz (source also of Old Frisian heil, Old Saxon, Old High German hagal, Old Norse hagl, German Hagel "hail"), probably from PIE *kaghlo- "pebble" (source also of Greek kakhlex "round pebble")..

meanings.definitionCn: 口音；重音；强调；特点；重音符号

etymology.story: "to pronounce with accent or stress," 1520s, from Middle French accenter, from Old French acenter, from acent (see accent (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 被动的，消极的；被动语态的

etymology.story: late 14c., in grammatical sense (opposed to active), Old French passif "suffering, undergoing hardship" (14c.) and directly from Latin passivus "capable of feeling or suffering," from pass-, past participle stem of pati "to suffer" (see passion).

1640s, from French granit(e) (17c.) or directly from Italian granito "granite," originally "grained," past participle adjective from granire "granulate, make grainy," from grano "grain," from Latin granum "grain" (see grain (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 使精力恢复的；使耳目一新的

etymology.story: 1570s, present participle adjective from refresh (v.).
mid-15c., from Latin obliviosus "forgetful, that easily forgets; producing forgetfulness," from oblivion (see oblivion).

meanings.definitionCn: 种类，部属，类目；范畴，类型

etymology.story: 1580s, from Middle French catégorie, from Late Latin categoria, from Greek kategoria "accusation, prediction, category," verbal noun from kategorein "to speak against; to accuse, assert, predicate," from kata "down to" (or perhaps "against;" see cata-) + agoreuein "to harangue, to declaim (in the assembly)," from agora "public assembly" (see agora).
